stem:
An 8-year-old boy presents to a tertiary paediatric ED on day 3 of a febrile illness with progressive painful rash. He was started on trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 9 days ago for recurrent UTI. Over the past 24 hours he has developed conjunctival redness with photophobia, painful oral erosions, dysphagia and dusky atypical targetoid macules on the trunk and face that are becoming confluent.

On examination temperature is 39.0 deg C, HR 140, RR 30, SpO2 96% RA, BP 95/55. There are haemorrhagic crusted lips, fibrinous oral mucosal erosions, conjunctival injection with serous discharge, urethral meatal erosion, and dusky atypical targetoid lesions over approximately 18 percent of body surface area with Nikolsky sign positive in two areas. Investigations show WCC 12 x10^9/L, CRP 70 mg/L, ALT 85 U/L, creatinine 90 micromol/L (baseline 50), Na 134 mmol/L, glucose 5.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L. He has no immune deficiency and is otherwise well-grown. The retrieval team and burns unit are en route.

data_table.Examination: Haemorrhagic crusted lips, fibrinous oral erosions, conjunctivitis, urethral erosion; dusky atypical targetoid lesions ~18% BSA; Nikolsky positive
data_table.Investigations: WCC 12, CRP 70, ALT 85, creatinine 90 (baseline 50), Na 134, HCO3 18

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management plan?

options.text: Cease trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole immediately, transfer to a centre with paediatric burns/intensive-care capability with ophthalmology and dermatology input, commence supportive care (fluid resuscitation, temperature control, analgesia, sterile non-adherent dressings, mucosal care), and document drug allergy and consider specialist-directed immunomodulation
options.rationale: SJS/TEN is a dermatological emergency - immediate withdrawal of the culprit drug is the single highest-impact intervention and improves mortality; supportive care equivalent to burns management, multidisciplinary input (paediatric ICU, burns, ophthalmology to prevent symblepharon, dermatology) and consideration of specialist-directed immunomodulation (e.g. ciclosporin, IVIG, TNF-alpha inhibitor) are the standard approach (eTG: Severe cutaneous adverse reactions; ANZBA paediatric SJS/TEN consensus; DermNet NZ: SJS).

options.text: Continue trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole at half dose, commence oral prednisolone daily with topical chlorhexidine 0.05% to mucosal surfaces twice daily, and admit for 24 hours of fluid balance and pain monitoring before reassessment
options.rationale: Continuing the culprit drug is unequivocally harmful in SJS/TEN; oral steroid monotherapy alone does not change mortality and chlorhexidine to denuded mucosa injures further. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: SCAR).

options.text: Treat as staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome with intravenous flucloxacillin six-hourly plus encourage oral hydration with maintenance fluids, paracetamol for fever and observation in the ED short-stay unit for 24 hours before discharge
options.rationale: Staphylococcal scalded skin occurs predominantly in young infants, has minimal mucosal involvement and intact mucosae; this case has temporal drug link plus mucositis. Would be correct in an infant with intact mucosae and positive culture (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Diagnose incomplete Kawasaki disease, commence intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 12 hours and high-dose aspirin in four divided doses with echocardiography on day 3 of admission to exclude coronary aneurysms
options.rationale: Kawasaki has polymorphous rash and mucosal change but not skin sloughing, Nikolsky sign or atypical targets, and lacks a culprit drug link. Would be correct with fever 5 days plus Kawasaki criteria (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki).

options.text: Perform a 4 mm punch biopsy of involved skin in ED, send for routine histology and direct immunofluorescence, and defer all therapy including culprit drug withdrawal and transfer until the histology result is available
options.rationale: Biopsy confirms but must not delay culprit withdrawal, transfer or supportive care in a mucocutaneous emergency. Would be correct as adjunct after initial stabilisation (Source: DermNet NZ: SJS).

explanation.summary: SJS/TEN management priorities are: immediate withdrawal of the culprit drug, transfer to a specialist centre with burns and PICU expertise, multidisciplinary support including ophthalmology to prevent ocular sequelae, supportive care and specialist-directed immunomodulation; culprit withdrawal is the single highest-impact intervention (eTG: Severe cutaneous adverse reactions).
key_points: Sulfa antibiotics, aromatic anticonvulsants, allopurinol, NSAIDs are common triggers (eTG; ASCIA).
Ophthalmology input within 24 hours reduces chronic ocular morbidity (DermNet NZ).
SCORTEN at 24 hours and 72 hours stratifies mortality risk (DermNet NZ).
explanation.pearls: Always document SJS/TEN drug allergy in the eHealth record (My Health Record) and on a medical alert bracelet - re-challenge can be lethal.

subtopic_detail: Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis early management in a child
Febrile infant under 3 months with possible UTI
stem: You are the paediatric registrar in a metropolitan tertiary emergency department reviewing a 7-week-old previously well term male infant brought in by his parents with a 12-hour history of poor feeding, fussiness and a measured rectal temperature of 38.6 degrees C at home. On examination he is irritable but consolable, well perfused with a capillary refill of under 2 seconds, has a soft fontanelle and no focal source of infection on full examination including ENT, chest, abdomen and skin. He has had no recent antibiotics and his immunisations are up to date.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4 degrees C, HR 168, RR 48, SpO2 99% air, CRT under 2 sec
data_table.Examination: Irritable but consolable, no rash, fontanelle soft, no meningism, normal genitalia
data_table.Investigations: Bedside cap gas: pH 7.38, BE -1, lactate 1.4 mmol/L, glucose 4.6 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Arrange an urgent renal tract ultrasound and a micturating cystourethrogram before starting any antibiotic therapy because vesicoureteric reflux is the most likely underlying diagnosis in a male infant with fever
options.rationale: Imaging is not done before empirical antibiotics; renal tract US in confirmed UTI is arranged later per KHA-CARI and RCH timelines. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Collect a clean-catch bag urine specimen for microscopy and culture and discharge home on oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg three times daily with a planned review by the general practitioner within 24 hours of presentation
options.rationale: Bag urine has a high contamination rate and is not acceptable for diagnosing UTI in infants; oral antibiotics and outpatient management are inappropriate for any febrile infant under 3 months. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

options.text: Reassure the parents that the child is well appearing with a normal lactate and observation alone in the emergency department for 4 hours with paracetamol is sufficient before discharge home without antibiotics or urine collection
options.rationale: Well-appearance is unreliable in infants under 3 months and serious bacterial infection prevalence is 5 to 10 percent; observation alone is inadequate. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

options.text: Send urine collected by clean catch only for dipstick analysis and start oral amoxicillin clavulanate if the dipstick is positive for leucocyte esterase or nitrites, deferring blood tests unless the child clinically deteriorates
options.rationale: Urine dipstick is unreliable in young infants and oral antibiotics are not first-line; full septic workup and IV antibiotics are mandated. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

options.text: Obtain a catheter or suprapubic aspirate urine sample plus blood culture and full septic workup including lumbar puncture, then commence intravenous benzylpenicillin and gentamicin pending results in a paediatric inpatient setting
options.rationale: RCH and the Therapeutic Guidelines treat a febrile infant under 3 months as full septic workup including catheter urine, blood culture, LP and empirical IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin (cefotaxime if LP delayed) regardless of well appearance. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

explanation.summary: Any febrile infant under 3 months of age requires a full septic workup. RCH defines this as urinalysis and culture by catheter or suprapubic aspirate, blood culture, FBC, CRP, and lumbar puncture (unless contraindicated). Empirical IV benzylpenicillin (or amoxicillin) plus gentamicin covers Group B streptococcus, Listeria and Gram negatives; cefotaxime replaces gentamicin if meningitis suspected. The infant is admitted pending cultures. Well-appearance does not exclude serious bacterial infection in this age group.
key_points: Under 1 month: always full septic workup plus admit on empirical IV antibiotics
1-3 months: full septic workup unless very well and low-risk per Rochester/Step-by-Step
Empirical IV penicillin plus gentamicin (cefotaxime if meningitis suspected)
Urine: catheter or SPA only, not bag
Renal tract US after confirmed UTI in infants under 6 months
explanation.pearls: Listeria monocytogenes covers the rationale for adding ampicillin/amoxicillin to gentamicin in neonates - it is intrinsically resistant to all cephalosporins.

subtopic_detail: Empiric oral antibiotic for first febrile UTI in well toddler
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing an 18-month-old girl in the short-stay unit with 24 hours of fever and reduced feeding. She is alert with no rash and tolerating sips. A clean-catch urine collected after meatal cleaning shows leucocyte esterase positive, nitrites positive, and microscopy with 200 white cells per microlitre. Local antibiogram shows E. coli trimethoprim resistance of 28 percent and cefalexin resistance of 6 percent.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7, HR 138, RR 28, SpO2 99 percent, BP 96/58
data_table.Examination: Alert, hydrated, soft non-tender abdomen, no flank tenderness, normal external genitalia
data_table.Investigations: Urine LE positive, nitrites positive, WCC 200/microL, urine culture pending; venous blood gas pH 7.38, lactate 1.4

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric oral antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg/dose twice daily for 7 days starting now
options.rationale: Amoxicillin clavulanate has broader gram negative cover than needed and higher rates of diarrhoea compared with the first-line agent. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until urine culture sensitivities are reported tomorrow morning
options.rationale: Delay in treatment of confirmed febrile UTI increases the risk of renal scarring; empiric therapy is started after the urine sample is taken. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 4 mg/kg/dose twice daily for 7 days starting now
options.rationale: Trimethoprim resistance of 28 percent locally exceeds the 20 percent ceiling for reliable empiric cover in febrile UTI. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg/dose twice daily for 7 days starting now
options.rationale: Cefalexin is the AU first-line oral agent for well children over 6 months with uncomplicated upper UTI given local resistance patterns and reliable oral absorption. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous gentamicin 7 mg/kg daily admit for 48 hours then oral switch
options.rationale: Intravenous therapy is reserved for children who are unwell, vomiting, under 3 months, or unable to tolerate oral feeds; this child is well enough for oral therapy. (Source: RCH CPG)

explanation.summary: Febrile UTI in young children is treated empirically after urine collection; the choice of oral agent depends on local resistance patterns and oral tolerance. AU practice favours cefalexin as a first-line agent when local E. coli resistance to trimethoprim is above the 20 percent ceiling. A 7 to 10 day oral course is standard for upper tract UTI in well children. Renal ultrasound is recommended after the first febrile UTI in children under 6 months and after atypical or recurrent infections in older children.
key_points: Empiric oral cefalexin first-line when trimethoprim resistance over 20 percent
IV therapy reserved for unwell, under 3 months, or vomiting
Renal ultrasound after first febrile UTI under 6 months or atypical features
Do not delay empirics for culture in symptomatic confirmed UTI
explanation.pearls: In Australian antibiograms most paediatric units now exceed the 20 percent trimethoprim resistance threshold, making oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg twice daily the default empiric for well febrile UTI.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 4-month-old girl admitted 3 days ago with her first febrile urinary tract infection. The catheter specimen grew Escherichia coli sensitive to her current IV antibiotic. She defervesced within 36 hours and is now feeding well, with normal observations. The bladder ultrasound performed during admission showed mild left renal pelvis dilatation but no hydroureter. The team is preparing her discharge plan and follow up.

data_table.Examination: Well, alert, feeding normally; abdomen soft, no masses
data_table.Investigations: Catheter urine: E. coli sensitive; bladder ultrasound: mild left pelvis dilatation, normal right; FBC and CRP normalised; UEC normal
data_table.Medications: IV gentamicin and amoxicillin during admission; switching to oral cefalexin for discharge

lead_in: What is the most appropriate further imaging plan?

options.text: Magnetic resonance urogram at 3 months to characterise the renal pelvis and exclude pelvi-ureteric junction obstruction
options.rationale: MR urography is a second-line modality used for selected complex anomalies, not the standard pathway after first febrile UTI with mild pelvis dilatation. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Routine MCUG within 2 weeks of discharge for every infant with a first febrile UTI regardless of imaging or clinical course
options.rationale: Routine MCUG for all first febrile UTIs is no longer recommended due to invasiveness, radiation and limited clinical benefit; selective use is the current standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months to assess for renal scarring; no MCUG unless recurrent UTI or abnormal DMSA
options.rationale: Australian and NICE-aligned guidance recommend DMSA at 4 to 6 months post first febrile UTI in infants under 3 years with ultrasound findings or atypical features; MCUG is reserved for recurrent UTI or abnormal DMSA. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Repeat bladder ultrasound only at 6 months with no further radiological investigation regardless of clinical course
options.rationale: Repeat ultrasound alone misses cortical scarring and reflux which are the key prognostic findings after febrile UTI in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Continuous prophylactic trimethoprim 2 mg/kg nocte for 12 months with no further imaging unless symptomatic
options.rationale: Prophylactic antibiotics are reserved for proven high-grade reflux or recurrent UTI; first-time UTI without proven reflux is not an indication for routine prophylaxis. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Australian first-febrile-UTI investigation in infants under 3 years uses a tiered pathway. Ultrasound during the acute admission identifies obstruction or major anomaly. DMSA at 4 to 6 months identifies cortical scarring. MCUG is reserved for recurrent UTI, abnormal DMSA, or atypical features (non-E. coli, septicaemia, poor response to 48 hours of treatment). Antibiotic prophylaxis is reserved for proven high-grade vesicoureteric reflux or recurrent UTI.
key_points: Acute admission: ultrasound for all under 3 years
DMSA at 4 to 6 months to assess scarring
MCUG reserved for recurrence, abnormal DMSA or atypical UTI
Antibiotic prophylaxis only for proven reflux or recurrence
explanation.pearls: An atypical UTI is defined as non-E. coli, septicaemia, raised creatinine, poor urine flow, abdominal mass or failure to respond to appropriate antibiotic within 48 hours.

subtopic_detail: Recognition and treatment of incomplete (atypical) Kawasaki disease in infants
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 9-month-old boy on day 6 of unexplained high fevers despite appropriate empiric ceftriaxone. He has a fine truncal rash, conjunctival injection without exudate, and red dry lips but no extremity changes, no cervical lymphadenopathy, and no oral cavity changes. Blood and urine cultures are sterile at 48 hours. Respiratory viral PCR is negative. Inflammatory markers are markedly elevated.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.2, HR 168, RR 38, SpO2 98 percent
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.4 x10^9/L, neutrophils 12.1 x10^9/L, platelets 540 x10^9/L, ALT 85 U/L, albumin 27 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue empiric ceftriaxone for a further 48 hours and review for clinical change
options.rationale: Persisting bacterial therapy alone delays Kawasaki recognition and increases the risk of coronary aneurysm beyond day 10. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Discharge with safety-net advice and arrange community paediatrician review in 1 week
options.rationale: Discharge before excluding Kawasaki disease in a febrile infant beyond day 5 is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Obtain echocardiography and treat with IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin given supplemental criteria are met
options.rationale: Infants under 12 months with prolonged unexplained fever and at least 3 supplemental laboratory criteria (CRP >=30, ESR >=40, anaemia, platelets >=450 after day 7, albumin <=30, sterile pyuria, raised ALT) meet incomplete Kawasaki criteria and receive IVIG plus aspirin; echo guides surveillance. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for 5 days as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Corticosteroid monotherapy is not first line; adjunctive steroids are considered alongside IVIG in high-risk children, not as a stand-alone. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Order a chest computed tomography to investigate alternative infectious aetiologies
options.rationale: CT chest does not address the most likely diagnosis and exposes the infant to unnecessary radiation. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is most likely in infants under 12 months and carries the highest coronary aneurysm risk if missed. Diagnosis requires fever for at least 5 days plus 2 or 3 of the classical criteria, with supplemental laboratory criteria substituting for absent clinical features. IVIG 2 g/kg as a single infusion plus aspirin (high or moderate dose) should be given by day 10 to minimise coronary risk. Echocardiography is performed at diagnosis, 2 weeks, and 6 to 8 weeks. High-risk infants may benefit from concomitant corticosteroid or infliximab.
key_points: Infants under 12 months often present incompletely and have the highest coronary risk
Supplemental criteria: CRP, ESR, albumin, anaemia, late platelet rise, pyuria, ALT
IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin by day 10 is the standard treatment
Echocardiography at baseline, 2 weeks, and 6 to 8 weeks
Adjunct steroid or infliximab is considered in high-risk infants
explanation.pearls: Sterile pyuria in a febrile infant beyond day 5 is a classic Kawasaki clue and should not be misattributed to a partially-treated urinary tract infection.

sources.label: American Heart Association: Kawasaki disease scientific statement

stem: A 6-month-old girl presents with fever of 38.8 C for 2 days, irritability, and reduced wet nappies. She has no prior UTI history. A catheter urine specimen confirms a urinary tract infection (pure growth of E. coli above 10^8 CFU/L). She responds well to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and is afebrile within 48 hours. This is her first documented UTI.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140 bpm, RR 34/min, temperature 38.8 C, weight 7.5 kg
data_table.Investigations: Urine MCS: E. coli >10^8 CFU/L (pure growth), sensitive to amoxicillin-clavulanate; FBC and CRP consistent with bacterial infection

lead_in: What is the most appropriate imaging investigation following treatment?

options.text: Renal tract ultrasound within 2 weeks of the acute infection
options.rationale: Australian guidelines (RCH, NICE) recommend a renal tract ultrasound for all children under 6 months with a first febrile UTI. The ultrasound assesses for structural abnormalities such as hydronephrosis, duplex collecting system, or renal scarring. It should be performed within 2 weeks of the acute episode. A micturating cystourethrogram (MCU) is not routinely required after a first uncomplicated UTI but should be considered if the ultrasound is abnormal. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection; NICE CG54 UTI in Children)

options.text: No imaging required as this is a first uncomplicated febrile UTI
options.rationale: While older children (over 3 years) with a first uncomplicated UTI may not require routine imaging, all children under 6 months with a febrile UTI should have at minimum a renal tract ultrasound. Structural abnormalities are more common and more clinically significant in younger infants. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection; NICE CG54 UTI in Children)

options.text: Micturating cystourethrogram as the first-line imaging investigation
options.rationale: MCU is an invasive test involving urethral catheterisation and fluoroscopy. It is not routinely indicated after a first uncomplicated UTI in an infant. It is reserved for recurrent UTIs, abnormal renal ultrasound findings, or atypical UTI presentation. Renal ultrasound is the appropriate first-line imaging. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

options.text: DMSA renal cortical scan performed at 6 months after the infection
options.rationale: DMSA scan is useful for detecting renal cortical scarring but is typically reserved for children with recurrent febrile UTIs, abnormal ultrasound, or atypical organisms. It is not the first-line investigation after a single uncomplicated febrile UTI with good clinical response. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection; NICE CG54 UTI in Children)

options.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast to exclude renal abscess
options.rationale: CT imaging involves significant radiation exposure in an infant and is not indicated after a straightforward UTI with good clinical response. CT is reserved for suspected complications such as perinephric abscess or when ultrasound findings are equivocal in the setting of a complicated UTI. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

explanation.summary: Investigation of paediatric UTIs is age-stratified. Infants under 6 months with a first febrile UTI should receive a renal tract ultrasound within 2 weeks. Children aged 6 months to 3 years with a typical first UTI and good response to treatment require ultrasound only if there are atypical features. Children over 3 years with a first uncomplicated UTI may not need imaging. MCU and DMSA scanning are reserved for recurrent UTIs, atypical organisms, poor response to treatment, or abnormal ultrasound findings. The goal is to identify structural abnormalities early in life when intervention is most effective.
key_points: Renal ultrasound within 2 weeks for all infants under 6 months with first febrile UTI
MCU is not routinely performed after a first uncomplicated UTI
DMSA scan for suspected renal scarring after recurrent UTIs or atypical presentation
Older children with typical first UTI may not require any imaging
explanation.pearls: The RCH guideline recommends MCU only after a first UTI if the renal ultrasound shows hydronephrosis, hydroureter, or other structural abnormality, or if the UTI was caused by an atypical organism (non-E. coli), or if the child had a poor clinical response (still febrile at 48 hours).

stem: A 21-day-old term female neonate presents to the emergency department with a temperature of 38.3 C measured rectally at home. She was born via normal vaginal delivery with no maternal risk factors for sepsis. She has been feeding adequately and has no rash. Her mother reports she has been slightly more sleepy than usual over the past 12 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 170 bpm, RR 48/min, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 38.5 C (rectal), CRT 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Mildly lethargic but rousable, anterior fontanelle soft and flat, no rash, no hepatosplenomegaly, mild tachypnoea

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Obtain blood and urine cultures, commence IV ampicillin and gentamicin
options.rationale: Any neonate under 28 days with fever (temperature 38.0 C or above) requires a full septic workup including blood cultures, urine culture (catheter specimen), and lumbar puncture, followed by empiric IV antibiotics. In neonates under 28 days, the recommended regimen is IV ampicillin (for Listeria and Enterococcus cover) plus IV gentamicin (for Gram-negative cover). Antibiotics should be commenced within 1 hour of presentation. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Obtain blood cultures only and observe for 6 hours before antibiotics
options.rationale: A febrile neonate under 28 days cannot be safely observed without empiric antibiotics. Serious bacterial infection occurs in approximately 10 to 12% of febrile neonates, and clinical appearance is unreliable in predicting which neonates are bacteraemic. Delaying antibiotics increases the risk of poor outcomes from unrecognised meningitis or septicaemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

options.text: Administer oral amoxicillin and arrange next-day GP follow-up
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate for a febrile neonate. The risk of serious bacterial infection in this age group is too high for outpatient management. All febrile neonates under 28 days require admission, IV antibiotics, and a full septic workup including lumbar puncture. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Obtain blood cultures and commence IV ceftriaxone as monotherapy
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates under 4 weeks due to the risk of biliary sludging and displacement of bilirubin from albumin. Additionally, cephalosporins do not cover Listeria monocytogenes or Enterococcus, which are important pathogens in this age group. Ampicillin plus gentamicin is the recommended empiric regimen. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

options.text: Check a urine dipstick and treat only if positive for nitrites
options.rationale: Urine dipstick is unreliable in neonates; many neonatal UTIs have negative nitrites because infant urinary flora may not produce nitrites. Furthermore, the concern in a febrile neonate is not limited to UTI - meningitis, pneumonia, and bacteraemia must all be considered. A full septic workup is mandatory. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child; RCH CPG: UTI)

explanation.summary: Fever in a neonate under 28 days is a clinical emergency. Approximately 10 to 12% of febrile neonates have a serious bacterial infection, and clinical appearance alone cannot reliably distinguish infected from non-infected infants. The standard approach is a full septic workup (blood cultures, catheter urine, lumbar puncture, chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms) followed by empiric IV antibiotics within 1 hour. The recommended empiric regimen for neonates under 28 days is IV ampicillin plus IV gentamicin, which covers the common pathogens (GBS, E. coli, Listeria, Enterococcus).
key_points: All febrile neonates under 28 days require full septic workup and empiric IV antibiotics
Empiric regimen: IV ampicillin plus IV gentamicin in neonates under 28 days
Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates under 4 weeks due to biliary sludging risk
Clinical appearance is unreliable for risk stratification in this age group
explanation.pearls: The Rochester criteria for low-risk febrile infants (well-appearing, no focal infection, WCC 5 to 15 x10^9/L, band count below 1.5 x10^9/L, normal urinalysis) can be applied to infants aged 29 to 60 days but are NOT applicable to neonates under 28 days, who always require full workup.

stem:
A 5-year-old boy is referred from his GP for evaluation of polyuria and polydipsia of 4 months' duration. His parents estimate his fluid intake at approximately 4 litres per day, including overnight water bottles, and he is waking 3-4 times nightly to drink and urinate, with secondary nocturnal enuresis after being dry at night for the previous 18 months. He has had a 1 kg weight loss but is otherwise growing on the 25th centile and his pubertal stage is Tanner 1 as expected.

There is no history of head injury, no headachesetes mellitus such as polyphagia. His past history includes Langerhans cell histiocytosis diagnosed 18 months ago at age 3 with cutaneous and bony involvement, treated with vinblastine and prednisolone, that has been in apparent remission on surveillance imaging. On examination he is alert, well perfused, with normal vital signs and an unremarkable systemic examination, including normal fundoscopy and visual field examination by confrontation.

Bloods today show Na 148 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, urea 6.2 mmol/L, creatinine 38 micromol/L, glucose 4.8 mmol/L, calcium 2.4 mmol/L, plasma osmolality 305 mOsm/kg with a paired urine osmolality of 110 mOsm/kg measured on a morning sample.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96 bpm, BP 104/64 mmHg, T 36.7 deg C, weight 18 kg
data_table.Examination: Alert, mucous membranes moist, no visual field defect, no papilloedema, no skin lesions visible
data_table.Investigations: Na 148 mmol/L, plasma osm 305 mOsm/kg, urine osm 110 mOsm/kg, SG 1.002, glucose 4.8 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Formal water deprivation test with paired plasma and urine osmolality, then desmopressin challenge
options.rationale: Water deprivation test risks dangerous dehydration when there is already biochemical evidence of inappropriate dilute urine in hypernatraemia; diagnosis is already established. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice: DI).
source_refs: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

options.text: Desmopressin trial with measurement of urine osmolality response, followed by MRI brain with pituitary protocol
options.rationale: When plasma is already hyperosmolar (>295 mOsm/kg) with hypernatraemia and inappropriately dilute urine (<300 mOsm/kg), DI is biochemically confirmed and a desmopressin trial (urine concentrates >50% = central DI; no response = nephrogenic DI) with MRI brain (looking for loss of posterior pituitary bright spot, infiltrative disease) is appropriate, particularly with this child's LCH history per RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus.
source_refs: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

options.text: Renal ultrasound and urinary tract DMSA scan to assess for medullary cystic disease
options.rationale: Renal imaging is reasonable later if nephrogenic DI is confirmed by no desmopressin response; not first-line discriminator. Would be correct after confirmed nephrogenic DI (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Oral glucose tolerance test to exclude type 1 diabetes mellitus as the cause
options.rationale: Random glucose 4.8 and urine SG 1.002 exclude osmotic diuresis; this is hypotonic polyuria not DM. Would be correct only with hyperglycaemia and glycosuria (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: 24-hour urinary calcium and serum parathyroid hormone to exclude hypercalciuric nephrogenic DI
options.rationale: Calcium normal at 2.4; chronic hypercalcaemia is unlikely primary cause here; LCH-related central DI is most likely given history. Would be correct only with hypercalcaemia (Source: ESPE).
source_refs: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

explanation.summary: Diabetes insipidus is suggested by hypotonic polyuria with hypernatraemia and hyperosmolar plasma (>295) with inappropriately dilute urine (<300). Once biochemically confirmed without need for water deprivation, a desmopressin trial distinguishes central (urine concentrates) from nephrogenic (no response). MRI brain is mandatory in confirmed central DI to exclude tumour, infiltration (LCH, germ cell tumour) or malformation.
key_points: Hypernatraemia should be corrected slowly (<10-12 mmol/L per 24 hours) to avoid cerebral oedema
Causes of central DI: idiopathic, tumour (craniopharyngioma, germinoma), LCH, trauma, infection
Nephrogenic DI: X-linked AVPR2 mutation, hypercalcaemia, hypokalaemia, lithium, sickle cell
Treatment of central DI: oral or intranasal desmopressin titrated to symptoms
explanation.pearls: LCH commonly involves the pituitary stalk and presents as central DI before any imaging-visible lesion. Repeat MRI 6-monthly if initial scan is normal.

sources.label: ESPE Consensus on Central Diabetes Insipidus

stem: A 5-week-old male infant is brought to ED with fever measured at 38.6 degrees Celsius at home and again on arrival. He was born at term by normal vaginal birth with no maternal GBS risk factors and has been feeding normally until today. He has fed less in the last 6 hours. He appears alert in the parents' arms but is mildly tachypnoeic without distress.

data_table.Examination: Alert, anterior fontanelle soft and flat, no rash, chest clear, abdomen soft; no focal infection; capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Investigations: Not yet done; needs evaluation per RCH SBI guideline

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate plan?

options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol and safety-net advice with paediatric review in 24 hours
options.rationale: All febrile infants under 3 months require full septic workup; outpatient management of fever in this age group is not safe. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile infant)

options.text: Take blood and urine cultures, lumbar puncture and commence IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin
options.rationale: AU first-line in fever under 6 weeks is full septic screen (FBC, CRP, blood culture, urine catheter sample, LP) plus empirical IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin pending cultures. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile infant; eTG)

options.text: Take blood cultures and CRP only and admit for 24 hours of observation without antibiotics
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are required in fever under 6 weeks even when the infant appears well; the consequences of missed bacterial meningitis are catastrophic. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile infant)

options.text: Perform urine bag sample only and commence oral amoxicillin awaiting outpatient review tomorrow
options.rationale: Urine bag samples have high contamination rates and oral antibiotics are inadequate; catheter or suprapubic urine plus IV antibiotics are required. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile infant)

options.text: Administer intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg single dose and discharge home for review
options.rationale: Single-dose IM ceftriaxone is not adequate empirical therapy in suspected neonatal sepsis under 6 weeks; admission for IV combination therapy is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile infant)

explanation.summary: Fever in infants under 3 months carries a 7 to 12 percent risk of serious bacterial infection (UTI most common, then bacteraemia, then meningitis), with poor predictive value of clinical appearance. AU first-line is full septic screen (FBC, CRP, blood culture, catheter or SPA urine, lumbar puncture in under 6 weeks) plus empirical antibiotics. Under 6 weeks: benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin; 6 weeks to 3 months: cefotaxime or benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin. CSF PCR for HSV is added if seizures, hepatitis, or maternal genital herpes.
key_points: All under 3 months with fever >= 38 degrees: septic workup + admission.
Under 6 weeks: full septic screen including LP; benzylpenicillin + gentamicin IV.
6 weeks to 3 months: blood + urine + selective LP per risk; IV antibiotics.
Catheter or SPA urine, NOT bag samples.
Add IV aciclovir if HSV risk factors (seizures, transaminitis, maternal HSV).
explanation.pearls: A 'fever-of-unknown-source' infant under 90 days with a positive urine culture still needs a normal CSF sample before stopping presumptive meningitis cover - around 5 percent of febrile UTIs in this age have concomitant meningitis.

subtopic_detail: First febrile UTI in young girl: imaging algorithm
stem: An 18-month-old girl is admitted with a first episode of fever to 39 degrees Celsius and vomiting. A clean catch urine culture grows greater than 10 to the 8 colony forming units per litre of Escherichia coli sensitive to trimethoprim. She responds to intravenous gentamicin with defervescence by 36 hours and switches to oral trimethoprim to complete a 7 day course. Family history is negative for renal disease.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130, RR 30, T 38.4 on day 2, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Well perfused, soft non-tender abdomen, no flank mass, normal external genitalia
data_table.Investigations: CRP peak 96 mg/L, creatinine 28 micromol/L, urine culture E. coli >10^8 CFU/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate further imaging plan after clinical recovery?

options.text: Urgent micturating cystourethrogram during the admission to exclude grade V vesicoureteric reflux
options.rationale: Routine MCUG is no longer recommended after a single uncomplicated UTI; it is selected for atypical illness or abnormal ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks of the acute illness with further imaging guided by findings
options.rationale: AU practice for a first febrile UTI in a child under 2 years is a renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks, with MCUG or DMSA reserved for atypical features or abnormal ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: DMSA renal scintigraphy at 6 weeks regardless of ultrasound findings to look for cortical scarring
options.rationale: DMSA is reserved for recurrent or atypical UTI or abnormal ultrasound, not routine for a single uncomplicated infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast to map the urinary tract
options.rationale: CT involves unnecessary radiation in young children and is not part of routine UTI follow up. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: No imaging is required; arrange continuous low dose trimethoprim prophylaxis for 12 months
options.rationale: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis without imaging is no longer recommended after a single uncomplicated febrile UTI. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Australian paediatric guidance for a first febrile urinary tract infection in a child under 2 years uses renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks as the screening investigation, looking for hydronephrosis, posterior urethral valves, duplex systems, or other structural abnormality. MCUG and DMSA are second-line and reserved for atypical UTI (non-Escherichia coli organism, septic illness, failure to respond within 48 hours, or abnormal voiding) or an abnormal ultrasound. Routine antibiotic prophylaxis after a single uncomplicated UTI is no longer recommended in the absence of high-grade reflux or other anatomical risk.
key_points: Renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks is first-line after a first febrile UTI in a child under 2 years
Atypical illness or abnormal ultrasound triggers MCUG or DMSA
Routine antibiotic prophylaxis after a single uncomplicated UTI is not supported by current evidence
explanation.pearls: An atypical UTI is one with seriously ill child, poor urine flow, abdominal mass, raised creatinine, septicaemia, non-Escherichia coli organism, or failure to respond within 48 hours, and prompts additional imaging.

sources.label: NICE NG224: Urinary tract infection in under 16s

subtopic_detail: Functional chronic constipation Movicol disimpaction and maintenance
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 4-year-old non-Aboriginal girl with a 14-month history of infrequent painful bowel motions, with stool frequency of 1 to 2 per week, frequent staining of underwear with small amounts of liquid stool between hard motions, and observed stool-withholding behaviour. There is a 6-month history of intermittent abdominal pain. She has met all developmental milestones, is fully toilet trained for urine, and has no symptoms of urinary tract infection. Examination shows a palpable suprapubic mass consistent with faecal loading, normal anal position and tone, no sacral dimple, and normal lower limb neurology.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 104, BP not routinely measured at age 4, weight 17 kg (50th centile)
data_table.Examination: Palpable suprapubic faecal mass, normal anal exam, no anal fissures, normal perineum and sacrum, normal lower limb neurology
data_table.Investigations: No bloods or imaging required for diagnosis of functional constipation

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase dietary fibre to 18 grams daily through fruit, vegetables and wholegrains, encourage 1.5 litres of fluid intake per day, and reassess at 4 weeks before considering any pharmacological intervention or specific oral stool-softening therapy
options.rationale: Dietary advice alone in the presence of demonstrable faecal impaction is insufficient; disimpaction with osmotic laxatives is recommended first to break the cycle. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric gastroenterology for anorectal manometry and rectal biopsy to exclude Hirschsprung's disease given the duration of symptoms and presence of overflow soiling at the age of presentation
options.rationale: Hirschsprung's disease typically presents in infancy with delayed meconium passage; functional constipation does not require biopsy unless red flags are present. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Administer paraffin oil 5 mL orally twice daily and arrange daily glycerine suppositories for 7 days followed by ongoing rectal interventions if dietary modifications fail to soften stool after this initial disimpaction phase of intensive treatment
options.rationale: Oral disimpaction with high-dose polyethylene glycol is preferred over invasive rectal interventions in children, which can be traumatic and worsen stool-withholding behaviour. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Disimpact with high-dose polyethylene glycol 3350 with electrolytes (Movicol Junior) 4 sachets daily, increasing to 8 sachets daily as needed for 3 to 7 days until impaction clears, then transition to maintenance Movicol 1 to 2 sachets daily with toileting routines and parent education
options.rationale: RCH guidance recommends oral disimpaction with high-dose Movicol Junior (escalating regimen until impaction clears) followed by maintenance therapy with sustained Movicol for 3+ months alongside behavioural toileting program. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Prescribe lactulose 5 mL twice daily for 3 months as the only intervention required for functional childhood constipation alongside oral fluid encouragement and gradual increase in dietary fibre over the next month
options.rationale: Disimpaction must precede maintenance; lactulose alone without disimpaction or behavioural intervention in the setting of faecal impaction is inadequate. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation in children is the cause of over 90% of presentations. Diagnosis is clinical (Rome IV criteria: 2+ of 2 or fewer defecations per week, history of retentive posturing, history of painful or hard motions, large diameter stools, faecal mass on exam, soiling in toilet-trained child). Red flags suggesting organic cause include delayed meconium past 48 hours, failure to thrive, fever, vomiting, abnormal anal exam or neurology, sacral abnormalities. Management is disimpaction with oral PEG (Movicol Junior, escalating doses), then maintenance for at least 3 months, plus toileting behavioural program. Avoid rectal interventions where possible.
key_points: Functional constipation: clinical diagnosis (Rome IV), 90% of cases
Red flags: delayed meconium, FTT, abnormal anal exam, neurology
Disimpaction: Movicol Junior 4-8 sachets daily over 3-7 days
Maintenance: Movicol 1-2 sachets daily for at least 3 months
Behavioural: regular toileting after meals, reward charts
explanation.pearls: Premature cessation of maintenance laxatives in the first 6 months is the single most common cause of relapse; counsel families that hard-stool memories take months to fade and that staying on maintenance through a painless interval is the actual treatment, not just a dose.

sources.label: NICE CG99: Constipation in children and young people

subtopic_detail: Persistent neonatal anuria suggesting posterior urethral valves
stem: A 3-day-old term male newborn has not passed urine since hospital discharge 24 hours after delivery and now appears irritable. His abdomen is mildly distended with a palpable bladder. Antenatal ultrasound at 32 weeks reported bilateral hydronephrosis but the parents declined further follow-up scans. His mother is otherwise well and birth was uncomplicated.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158, RR 52, T 36.7, weight 3.2 kg (down 9 percent from birth weight)
data_table.Examination: Palpable bladder to umbilicus, no abdominal mass, normal external genitalia, dribbling urine stream when expressed
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 168 micromol/L, Na 132, K 6.4, bedside USS distended bladder, bilateral hydronephrosis, dilated posterior urethra

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Suprapubic aspirate of urine for culture and discharge home with outpatient nephrology review
options.rationale: Aspiration alone does not relieve bladder outlet obstruction; ongoing obstruction continues to damage the kidneys and bladder. (Source: RCH CPG Posterior Urethral Valves)

options.text: Fluid challenge with normal saline 20 mL per kg then reassess for spontaneous voiding within 4 hours
options.rationale: Volume loading without relieving obstruction risks bladder injury and worsening hydronephrosis; catheter decompression is required first. (Source: RCH CPG Posterior Urethral Valves)

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 1 mg per kg to promote urine output before any imaging is performed
options.rationale: Frusemide does not relieve mechanical obstruction; outflow must be established first by catheterisation, and dehydration may worsen the picture. (Source: RCH CPG Posterior Urethral Valves)

options.text: Urgent micturating cystourethrogram first before any other intervention to confirm the diagnosis
options.rationale: MCUG is required to confirm the diagnosis but only after the bladder is decompressed and the baby is stabilised; not the first step in an obstructed septic-risk neonate. (Source: RCH CPG Posterior Urethral Valves)

options.text: Insert a small-bore urethral catheter and admit to neonatal unit with urology referral
options.rationale: Suspected posterior urethral valves are decompressed urgently with bladder catheterisation, then transferred for paediatric urological assessment and surgical valve ablation. (Source: RCH CPG Posterior Urethral Valves)

explanation.summary: Posterior urethral valves are the commonest cause of severe lower urinary tract obstruction in male infants. Antenatal findings include bilateral hydronephrosis, dilated bladder and keyhole sign of dilated posterior urethra. Postnatally, suspect in any boy with weak stream, palpable bladder or anuria. Immediate management is catheter decompression with a small feeding tube (urethral catheter balloons can lodge in dilated posterior urethra), correction of electrolytes and prophylactic antibiotics. Definitive surgery is cystoscopic valve ablation. Long-term complications include chronic kidney disease, bladder dysfunction and need for transplantation in around 30 percent.
key_points: Suspect in boy with weak stream + palpable bladder + AKI.
Catheter decompression first - use feeding tube without balloon.
Send creatinine, electrolytes, USS post-decompression.
Cystoscopic valve ablation is definitive.
Long-term CKD risk - lifelong urology and nephrology follow-up.
explanation.pearls: Post-obstructive diuresis is common after relief of bilateral obstruction - replace urine output 1:1 with 0.45 percent saline plus dextrose for 24 to 48 hours to prevent profound electrolyte and volume disturbance.

sources.label: Australian and New Zealand Society for Paediatric Surgery

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a previously well 5-month-old girl who has been admitted with her first febrile urinary tract infection. She responded to intravenous cefotaxime within 48 hours and is now afebrile and feeding well. Urine culture grew Escherichia coli over 100 x10^6 CFU/L sensitive to trimethoprim and cephalexin. Antenatal scans were normal and her parents have no family history of vesicoureteric reflux or renal disease.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 132, RR 32, SpO2 99% room air, BP 88/52 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused, soft non-tender abdomen, no palpable bladder or flank mass, normal genitalia
data_table.Investigations: Mid-stream clean catch urine: leucocytes 250/microL, nitrites positive, culture E. coli over 100 x10^6 CFU/L cefotaxime sensitive; FBC, UEC and CRP within paediatric reference ranges after treatment

lead_in: What is the most appropriate imaging plan from here?

options.text: Arrange a renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks of discharge and no further imaging unless the ultrasound is abnormal or she has a recurrent febrile urinary tract infection in the next 12 months
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months require ultrasound during the acute illness or shortly afterwards rather than at 6 weeks. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Arrange a renal tract ultrasound during this admission and add a micturating cystourethrogram only if the ultrasound shows hydronephrosis, scarring or recurrent febrile urinary tract infection occurs subsequently
options.rationale: Australian paediatric guidance recommends ultrasound during the index admission for infants under 6 months with febrile UTI; MCUG is reserved for abnormal ultrasound, atypical UTI or recurrence. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Arrange a DMSA renogram within 4 weeks of discharge and a micturating cystourethrogram for all infants under 12 months after a first febrile urinary tract infection regardless of severity
options.rationale: Routine MCUG and DMSA after a single uncomplicated first febrile UTI in a treatment-responsive infant is not recommended in Australia. (Source: KHA-CARI Adolescent Paediatric UTI)

options.text: Arrange computed tomography of the urinary tract during this admission to exclude calculus and complex anatomical anomaly given the high colony count growth and the young age of the infant
options.rationale: CT is not first-line imaging in paediatric UTI because of radiation exposure; ultrasound is the appropriate modality. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Commence long-term low-dose trimethoprim prophylaxis at 2 mg/kg nocte and defer all renal tract imaging until age 2 years unless she develops a further breakthrough febrile urinary tract infection
options.rationale: Prophylaxis is reserved for recurrent UTI or significant reflux; deferring all imaging in an infant under 6 months misses tract anomalies that need follow-up. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Infants under 6 months with their first febrile UTI need a renal tract ultrasound during or shortly after the admission to look for obstruction, hydronephrosis or duplex anatomy. Routine MCUG and DMSA are not required after an uncomplicated treatment-responsive first UTI; both are added selectively for atypical features, abnormal ultrasound or recurrence. Long-term prophylaxis after a first uncomplicated UTI is not recommended. Children with confirmed high-grade reflux or significant scarring need nephrology input.
key_points: Ultrasound during admission is standard for infants under 6 months with febrile UTI
MCUG is reserved for abnormal ultrasound, atypical UTI or recurrence
DMSA is selective and not routine after a first uncomplicated UTI
CT and IV urogram are not first-line in paediatric UTI
Long-term prophylaxis is targeted, not universal
explanation.pearls: Atypical UTI features that escalate imaging include seriously unwell appearance, poor urine flow, non-E. coli organism, raised creatinine, sepsis or failure to respond within 48 hours of appropriate antibiotics.

subtopic_detail: Febrile UTI in infants under 3 months
stem: A 6-week-old infant girl is brought to your emergency department by her parents because of a tactile fever today, feeding refusal and increased sleepiness. She has not had any cough, coryza or rash. She was born at term, has been growing normally on the 50th centile, and was vaccinated at 6 weeks two days ago. On examination she has a temperature of 38.4 degrees Celsius rectally, is mildly irritable when handled and has no obvious focus of infection on careful examination including ears, throat and chest.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, BP 86/54, RR 48, SpO2 98% on RA, Temp 38.4 rectal
data_table.Examination: Mildly irritable; AF flat; no rash; no respiratory distress; no focus on examination
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.4; CRP 38; venous pH 7.36, lactate 2.4; urine pending; chest X-ray clear

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Send a bag urine sample for culture and discharge home with safety-net advice and paediatric follow-up at the local general practice clinic in 24 hours
options.rationale: Febrile infants under 3 months are at high risk of bacteraemia and meningitis and require admission and full septic workup; bag urine has high contamination rates and is not used. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

options.text: Admit, take blood cultures, lumbar puncture and a clean catch or suprapubic aspirate urine, then commence intravenous benzylpenicillin plus cefotaxime empirically as a septic workup approach
options.rationale: Febrile infants under 3 months require full septic workup including blood cultures, lumbar puncture and catheter or suprapubic aspirate urine, with immediate empirical intravenous antibiotics pending cultures regardless of clinical appearance. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

options.text: Reassure family that her recent vaccination explains the fever and observe for 4 hours without bloods, urine, lumbar puncture or empirical antibiotic therapy at the present time
options.rationale: Vaccine-associated fever does not explain fever above 38 degrees in a 6-week-old; serious bacterial infection must be excluded before attribution to vaccination. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

options.text: Perform a urinalysis only and treat as a presumed urinary tract infection with oral cefalexin for ten days while awaiting culture confirmation from the bag sample collected today
options.rationale: Empirical oral antibiotics for an infant under 3 months without lumbar puncture and blood cultures risks missing meningitis or bacteraemia; intravenous antibiotics in hospital are required. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

options.text: Order a chest X-ray and respiratory viral PCR only to identify the likely viral cause of the fever before deciding on the need for any antibiotic therapy in this infant
options.rationale: Identifying a viral cause does not eliminate the risk of serious bacterial infection in this age group; full septic workup and empirical antibiotics are still required. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

explanation.summary: All febrile infants under 3 months require admission for full septic workup including blood culture, lumbar puncture and catheter or suprapubic aspirate urine, with empirical intravenous antibiotic cover for likely pathogens (commonly benzylpenicillin plus cefotaxime, plus aciclovir if HSV is suspected). Bag urine is unsuitable for diagnosis because of contamination. UTI is the single most common serious bacterial infection in this age group and meningitis must be actively excluded. Risk stratification (Rochester, Step-by-Step) may be applied to selected well-appearing infants but is not used to defer workup at this age.
key_points: All febrile infants under 3 months: admit, full septic workup, empirical IV antibiotics
Catheter or suprapubic aspirate urine; bag urine not used
Lumbar puncture mandatory before empirical antibiotics where feasible
Empirical cover: benzylpenicillin plus cefotaxime
explanation.pearls: For any febrile (>=38.0) infant under 3 months, admit and complete blood culture plus lumbar puncture plus clean-catch or suprapubic aspirate urine before commencing empirical intravenous benzylpenicillin plus cefotaxime rather than discharging on oral antibiotics.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 10-month-old boy admitted overnight with first febrile UTI. Urine grew E. coli sensitive to cefalexin. He responded to IV ceftriaxone within 24 hours and has been afebrile for 48 hours. He is now feeding well, hydrated, and ready for discharge on oral cefalexin to complete the course. The parents ask about follow-up imaging.

data_table.Examination: Well, no abdominal mass, normal genitalia
data_table.Investigations: Urine culture E. coli sensitive to cefalexin; bedside renal tract ultrasound during admission was normal
data_table.Medications: Oral cefalexin to complete a total 10-day course

lead_in: What further imaging or investigation is most appropriate?

options.text: Arrange a DMSA scan at three months to assess for renal scarring after the first febrile UTI
options.rationale: DMSA is recommended after atypical or recurrent UTIs, not after a first uncomplicated febrile UTI with normal ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: Arrange a micturating cystourethrogram in the next four weeks to exclude vesicoureteric reflux
options.rationale: MCUG is reserved for atypical UTI, recurrent UTI, or abnormal ultrasound findings, not for a first uncomplicated UTI that responded promptly. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: No further imaging is required as the renal tract ultrasound during admission was normal
options.rationale: For a first febrile UTI under 24 months with prompt response to first-line therapy and a normal ultrasound, no further imaging is required. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: Commence trimethoprim antibiotic prophylaxis until two years of age as routine secondary prevention
options.rationale: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis after a single uncomplicated UTI is not recommended due to limited benefit and resistance. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: Repeat a urine culture at the end of the antibiotic course to confirm microbiological cure
options.rationale: Test of cure is not required if the child is clinically well at the end of therapy. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

explanation.summary: Australian guidance from RCH stratifies post-UTI imaging by atypia and recurrence. A first febrile UTI in a child under 24 months who responds promptly and has a normal renal ultrasound needs no further imaging. MCUG and DMSA are reserved for atypical features or recurrence. The distractors test over-investigation patterns common in inherited NICE-derived practice.
key_points: Renal tract ultrasound during admission is standard for febrile UTI under 24 months
Atypical features include non-E coli organism, poor response by 48 hours, abdominal mass, or sepsis
Prophylaxis is not first-line after a single uncomplicated UTI
explanation.pearls: RCH UTI pathway: ultrasound during admission for under-24-months febrile UTI; MCUG only if atypical or recurrent or ultrasound abnormal.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: UTI in children

subtopic_detail: Primary nocturnal enuresis - management after alarm failure
stem: A 9-year-old boy presents with ongoing primary nocturnal enuresis. He wets the bed 5 nights per week and has never had a sustained dry period. He has no daytime symptoms, no constipation, and no urinary tract infections. A 3-month trial of an enuresis alarm was attempted with good compliance but produced no improvement. His urinalysis is normal and postvoid residual is negligible on ultrasound.

data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: specific gravity 1.020, no protein, no glucose, no leucocytes, no nitrites. Renal tract ultrasound: normal kidneys and bladder, postvoid residual < 10 mL.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat the enuresis alarm trial for a further 3 months
options.rationale: An adequate alarm trial is 6-8 weeks with good compliance. This child has had a 3-month trial with no improvement, indicating alarm therapy failure. Repeating the same intervention is unlikely to succeed and delays alternative treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Nocturnal Enuresis)

options.text: Fluid restriction after 5pm with scheduled overnight waking
options.rationale: Evening fluid restriction alone has limited evidence for efficacy and scheduled overnight waking is disruptive without evidence of sustained benefit. After alarm failure, pharmacotherapy is the recommended next step. (Source: NICE: Nocturnal Enuresis)

options.text: Oral desmopressin melt at bedtime for an initial 3-month trial
options.rationale: After alarm therapy failure, desmopressin (DDAVP) is the second-line treatment. The sublingual melt formulation is preferred in children. A 3-month trial with structured withdrawal (gradual dose reduction) is recommended. Response is expected within 2 weeks. Fluid restriction 1 hour before to 8 hours after dosing is essential to avoid hyponatraemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Nocturnal Enuresis; AMH Children's Dosing Companion)

options.text: Oral oxybutynin for overactive bladder with enuresis component
options.rationale: Oxybutynin is indicated for enuresis with daytime overactive bladder symptoms such as urgency, frequency, or daytime wetting. This child has no daytime symptoms, making anticholinergic therapy inappropriate as monotherapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Nocturnal Enuresis)

options.text: Referral for urodynamic studies and specialist urology review
options.rationale: Urodynamic studies are reserved for children with complex lower urinary tract symptoms, daytime incontinence unresponsive to treatment, or suspected neuropathic bladder. A child with isolated monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis and normal investigations does not require urodynamics at this stage. (Source: RCH CPG: Nocturnal Enuresis)

explanation.summary: Primary nocturnal enuresis is involuntary night-time bedwetting in a child over 5 years who has never achieved sustained dryness. It is monosymptomatic when there are no daytime lower urinary tract symptoms. First-line treatment is an enuresis alarm, which has the highest long-term cure rate. When alarm therapy fails after an adequate trial, desmopressin is recommended as second-line. Desmopressin reduces nocturnal urine production and has rapid onset, but relapse rates are higher than alarm therapy once discontinued.
key_points: Enuresis alarm is first-line with a 70% success rate but requires 6-8 weeks of consistent use
Desmopressin is second-line after alarm failure, with response expected within 1-2 weeks
Structured withdrawal of desmopressin over 2-4 weeks reduces relapse rates compared to abrupt cessation
Fluid restriction from 1 hour before to 8 hours after desmopressin is mandatory to prevent hyponatraemia
Oxybutynin is reserved for enuresis with daytime overactive bladder symptoms
explanation.pearls: Desmopressin-associated hyponatraemia is rare but potentially fatal - fluid intake must be restricted from 1 hour before dosing to 8 hours after, and the drug must be withheld during intercurrent illnesses with vomiting or excessive fluid intake.

subtopic_detail: Acute scrotal pain - testicular torsion exploration
stem: A 14-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with sudden onset severe left scrotal pain that began 3 hours ago during a basketball game. He has vomited twice and the pain radiates to the left groin. He denies urinary symptoms, penile discharge, or recent trauma. He has no prior episodes.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, BP 125/75 mmHg, Temp 37.1 C
data_table.Examination: Left testis high-riding and transversely oriented. Absent cremasteric reflex on the left. Exquisitely tender left hemiscrotum with a firm, swollen testis. Right testis normal. No erythema of scrotal skin. Negative Prehn sign.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: no leucocytes, no nitrites, no blood.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Urgent scrotal Doppler ultrasound to confirm diagnosis
options.rationale: When clinical features are highly suggestive of testicular torsion, ultrasound should not delay surgical exploration. Testicular salvage rates drop significantly after 6 hours of ischaemia, and the time spent obtaining imaging may cross this threshold. Ultrasound sensitivity for torsion is also not 100%. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotal Pain)

options.text: Immediate surgical exploration and orchidopexy
options.rationale: This presentation has the classic features of testicular torsion: sudden severe pain, high-riding transversely oriented testis, absent cremasteric reflex, and negative Prehn sign. With 3 hours of symptom duration, immediate surgical exploration offers the best chance of testicular salvage. Bilateral orchidopexy (fixation of both testes) is performed at the same time. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotal Pain; Therapeutic Guidelines: Surgery)

options.text: Manual detorsion attempt with analgesia in the ED
options.rationale: Manual detorsion (lateral rotation of the affected testis - 'open the book') can be attempted as a temporising measure but is not definitive management. Even if successful, surgical orchidopexy is still required to prevent recurrence. With theatre access available within the salvage window, proceeding directly to exploration is preferred. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotal Pain)

options.text: IV antibiotics for presumed epididymo-orchitis
options.rationale: Epididymo-orchitis is uncommon in prepubertal and early-adolescent boys without urinary tract abnormalities. The clinical features here (sudden onset, high-riding testis, absent cremasteric reflex, clear urinalysis) strongly favour torsion over infection. Treating for infection delays surgical exploration. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotal Pain)

options.text: Analgesia and repeat examination in 2 hours
options.rationale: Expectant management is dangerous in suspected testicular torsion. Testicular salvage rates are approximately 90% within 6 hours but drop to less than 10% after 24 hours. A 2-hour delay could push this patient beyond the optimal salvage window. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotal Pain)

explanation.summary: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency with a bimodal peak in neonates and adolescents (12-18 years). The pathognomonic finding is a high-riding, transversely oriented testis with an absent cremasteric reflex. When clinical suspicion is high, immediate surgical exploration should not be delayed for imaging. Testicular salvage rates are strongly time-dependent, with near-complete salvage within 6 hours declining rapidly thereafter. Bilateral orchidopexy is performed during exploration to prevent contralateral torsion.
key_points: Absent cremasteric reflex has the highest sensitivity (99%) among individual clinical signs for testicular torsion
Testicular salvage rate is approximately 90% within 6 hours, 50% at 12 hours, and less than 10% after 24 hours
Ultrasound should not delay exploration when clinical probability of torsion is high
Bilateral orchidopexy is performed at the time of exploration regardless of whether the testis is viable
Torsion of a testicular appendage (hydatid of Morgagni) is the most common differential and shows a blue dot sign
explanation.pearls: The cremasteric reflex is elicited by stroking the inner thigh and observing ipsilateral testicular elevation - its absence in a boy with acute scrotal pain has a sensitivity of 99% for testicular torsion and should prompt immediate surgical referral without imaging.

stem: A 14-month-old girl has had two febrile urinary tract infections in 6 months, both caused by Escherichia coli. After her second episode, a micturating cystourethrogram (MCUG) is performed and shows bilateral vesicoureteral reflux - grade II on the right and grade IV on the left with moderate left ureteric dilatation and blunting of the left renal calyces. A DMSA scan shows a 15% differential function defect in the left kidney with cortical scarring at the upper pole.

data_table.Investigations: MCUG: right VUR grade II, left VUR grade IV. DMSA scan: left kidney 35% differential function (normal > 45%), upper pole cortical scar. Right kidney 65% differential function, normal uptake.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Observation with prompt treatment of future symptomatic UTIs
options.rationale: Observation alone may be appropriate for low-grade VUR (grades I-II) without renal scarring. This child has grade IV reflux with documented renal scarring and recurrent febrile UTIs, warranting a more proactive approach to prevent further renal damage. (Source: RCH CPG: Vesicoureteral Reflux; KHA-CARI Guidelines)

options.text: High-dose intermittent antibiotic therapy for acute episodes only
options.rationale: Treating only acute episodes without prophylaxis exposes the scarred kidney to further damage with each febrile UTI. In a child with high-grade reflux and existing renal scarring, the risk of progressive renal damage from recurrent pyelonephritis is significant. (Source: KHA-CARI Guidelines)

options.text: Bilateral ureteric reimplantation surgery within 4 weeks
options.rationale: Surgical reimplantation is effective for high-grade VUR but is not the immediate first step. A trial of antibiotic prophylaxis is generally recommended first, with surgery reserved for breakthrough febrile UTIs on prophylaxis, worsening renal scarring, or non-resolution of high-grade reflux. The grade II right side is also unlikely to require surgery. (Source: RCH CPG: Vesicoureteral Reflux)

options.text: Endoscopic subureteric injection of bulking agent bilaterally
options.rationale: Endoscopic injection (e.g. Deflux) is an option for VUR but has lower success rates for grade IV-V reflux compared with open reimplantation. It is not the recommended first-line approach before trialling antibiotic prophylaxis, especially for high-grade reflux with renal scarring. (Source: RCH CPG: Vesicoureteral Reflux)

options.text: Continuous low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis with regular follow-up
options.rationale: For a young child with recurrent febrile UTIs, grade IV VUR, and existing renal scarring, continuous antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g. trimethoprim) is recommended to reduce UTI recurrence while allowing time for spontaneous resolution. Regular follow-up includes repeat MCUG at 12-18 months and repeat DMSA if breakthrough UTIs occur. If breakthrough febrile UTIs occur on prophylaxis, surgical options should be discussed. (Source: RCH CPG: Vesicoureteral Reflux; KHA-CARI Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Vesicoureteral reflux management depends on grade, presence of renal scarring, and frequency of breakthrough UTIs. Low-grade VUR (I-III) without scarring often resolves spontaneously and may be managed conservatively. High-grade VUR (IV-V) with renal scarring carries a higher risk of progressive renal damage and warrants continuous antibiotic prophylaxis. Surgical intervention (open reimplantation or endoscopic injection) is reserved for cases failing medical management. Serial imaging monitors for reflux resolution and new scarring.
key_points: VUR is graded I-V based on the degree of ureteric dilatation and calyceal blunting on MCUG
Antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended for high-grade VUR with renal scarring to prevent further damage
Spontaneous resolution rates decrease with increasing VUR grade: approximately 80% for grade I-II vs 30% for grade IV
DMSA scan is the gold standard for detecting renal cortical scarring
Surgical reimplantation is reserved for breakthrough febrile UTIs on prophylaxis or non-resolving high-grade reflux
explanation.pearls: Trimethoprim at one-third of the therapeutic dose given as a single nightly dose is the standard prophylactic regimen for VUR in Australian practice - nitrofurantoin is an alternative but is avoided in infants under 1 month due to haemolytic risk.

stem:
A 5-week-old term male infant is brought to a regional ED by his parents after a single rectal temperature of 38.4 deg C taken because he was unsettled and feeding poorly for the last 8 hours. He was born by normal vaginal delivery at 39+2 weeks with no risk factors for early-onset sepsis: maternal GBS swab negative at 36 weeks, no prolonged rupture of membranes, no intrapartum fever.

He had an unremarkable nursery course and went home on day 2. He is exclusively breastfed and has been gaining weight along the 50th centile. He has had no cough, no diarrhoea, and there are no sick contacts. He has not yet received any immunisations as his 6-week appointment is next week. On examination he is mottled peripherally, with a soft anterior fontanelle, no focus of infection, and a capillary refill of 3 seconds. He has been crying weakly but is consolable.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4 deg C rectal, HR 184 bpm, RR 56 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, BP 76/42 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Mottled peripheries, CRT 3 s, anterior fontanelle soft and flat, no focal infection, no rash, weak cry
data_table.History: Term NVD, GBS negative, no maternal fever, exclusively breastfed, not yet immunised

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen after blood, urine and CSF are obtained?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg eight-hourly as initial empirical monotherapy
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates and very young infants (bilirubin displacement, calcium incompatibility); cefotaxime is preferred. Would be correct in over-1-month infants (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV ampicillin 50 mg/kg plus IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg
options.rationale: In febrile infants under 1 month (and many units extend to under 3 months) empirical cover must include Listeria monocytogenes and group B Streptococcus (ampicillin) plus broad Gram-negative and meningitis cover (cefotaxime). Ceftriaxone is avoided in this age group, per RCH CPG: Febrile child and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg plus benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin doesn't cover Listeria as monotherapy; triple therapy is not standard. AU neonatal sepsis cover is ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) plus gentamicin with cefotaxime if meningitis suspected. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg plus IV gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg eight-hourly
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin plus gentamicin does not cover Listeria, GBS or many Gram-negative urinary pathogens, and lacks CSF penetration. Would be correct only in suspected staph osteomyelitis without sepsis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg and review in 4 hours pending blood results
options.rationale: Any febrile infant under 3 months requires full septic workup and parenteral antibiotics until cultures negative at 36-48 h. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child).

explanation.summary: Fever in an infant under 3 months mandates a full septic workup (blood, urine via SPA or catheter, CSF) and empirical IV antibiotics. Under 1 month: ampicillin plus cefotaxime (add gentamicin if shocked or suspected UTI). 1 to 3 months: cefotaxime or ceftriaxone, with ampicillin still added if Listeria risk persists per local guideline.
key_points: Listeria and group B Strep mandate ampicillin in young infants
Ceftriaxone avoided in neonates (bilirubin displacement, calcium incompatibility)
CSF should be obtained before antibiotics unless contraindicated
Cefotaxime dose 50 mg/kg q6h for meningitis
Continue antibiotics until cultures negative at 36 to 48 hours
explanation.pearls: If you cannot remember whether to add ampicillin, ask the infant's age in weeks: under 6 weeks always covers Listeria.

subtopic_detail: Sepsis evaluation in the febrile young infant

stem: A 2-year-old boy is recovering from his second culture-confirmed febrile UTI in 6 months, both with E. coli. His first UTI at 18 months was treated with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and he had a normal renal tract ultrasound at the time. Today he is afebrile after 24 hours of oral co-trimoxazole. His mother asks what additional imaging is needed.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.0 degrees Celsius, HR 110, RR 24, BP 96/58 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Well, abdomen soft non-tender, no renal angle tenderness, growth on 50th centile, no spinal anomaly

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate imaging plan?

options.text: Commence long-term low-dose trimethoprim prophylaxis and defer imaging until a third infection occurs
options.rationale: Prophylaxis alone without DMSA misses scarring; Australian guidance is to assess cortical status before deciding on long-term prophylaxis. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Arrange urgent micturating cystourethrogram now to assess for vesico-ureteric reflux before discharge
options.rationale: MCUG is invasive and reserved for abnormal DMSA or ultrasound or atypical infection; routine MCUG after a second uncomplicated UTI is no longer recommended. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Repeat the renal tract ultrasound during this admission to look for new structural abnormality
options.rationale: A normal recent ultrasound does not need to be repeated in the same year unless clinical features have changed; DMSA carries the diagnostic yield for scarring. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Arrange CT KUB with intravenous contrast to characterise renal cortex and exclude obstructive uropathy
options.rationale: CT delivers significant radiation and is not used to screen for scarring or reflux; DMSA is the standard test for cortical scarring in children. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Arrange a DMSA scan in 4 to 6 months to assess for renal cortical scarring after this second UTI
