options.rationale: After a second febrile UTI in a child under 3 years, Australian guidelines recommend DMSA at 4 to 6 months after the most recent infection to assess for cortical scarring, with renal tract ultrasound already done. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: After a second febrile UTI in a child under 3 years, Australian guidelines recommend a DMSA scan 4 to 6 months after the most recent infection to assess for cortical scarring, with renal tract ultrasound already performed. Routine MCUG is no longer recommended and is reserved for atypical infection or abnormal DMSA or ultrasound findings. CT has no routine role.
key_points: DMSA at 4 to 6 months is the standard scarring assessment after recurrent febrile UTI
MCUG is reserved for atypical infection or abnormal scarring or anatomy
Ultrasound is done acutely for atypical infection and at first UTI under 6 months
explanation.pearls: Bottom-up imaging strategies (ultrasound, then DMSA, then MCUG only if abnormal) have reduced unnecessary invasive imaging without missing significant uropathy.

sources.label: NICE NG: Urinary tract infection in under 16s

stem:
A 10-month-old female infant (weight 9.2 kg) presents to her GP with 4 days of intermittent fever to 39 deg C, irritability, and reduced feeding. She has had no cough, coryza, vomiting, or diarrhoea, and no rash. She has no known urinary tract anomalies on the routine antenatal scans. She is fully immunised. A clean-catch urine collected at the GP and sent to the local pathology lab shows leucocytes 3+, nitrites positive, and a pure growth of E.

coli > 10^8 CFU/L sensitive to trimethoprim and cephalexin. Renal tract ultrasound has not yet been performed. She is now afebrile after a paracetamol dose and feeding more comfortably; the GP is deciding on follow-up imaging and prophylaxis.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 deg C, HR 130 bpm, RR 30 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 92/58 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Alert and interactive, abdomen soft and non-tender, no flank tenderness elicitable, capillary refill < 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Urine MCS: leucocytes 3+, nitrites positive, E. coli > 10^8 CFU/L; FBC and UEC not performed; growth velocity normal on chart
data_table.History: First UTI, fully immunised, no antenatal urinary tract anomaly, breastfed plus solids

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step regarding imaging and follow-up?

options.text: Arrange a micturating cystourethrogram (MCUG) within 6 weeks to assess for vesicoureteric reflux and posterior urethral valves
options.rationale: MCUG is not first-line after a first febrile UTI in an infant; it is reserved for atypical or recurrent UTI or abnormal ultrasound per RCH CPG: UTI.

options.text: Arrange a renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks and consider further imaging only if abnormal
options.rationale: After a first febrile UTI in an infant under 2 years, renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks is recommended; further imaging (MCUG, DMSA) depends on the ultrasound findings and clinical course per RCH CPG: UTI and NICE guidance.

options.text: No further imaging required as the urinary tract infection has resolved with oral antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Imaging is indicated after a first febrile UTI in this age group to detect structural abnormalities and reflux per RCH CPG: UTI.

options.text: Commence long-term prophylactic trimethoprim nocte for 12 months pending nephrology outpatient review
options.rationale: Routine prophylaxis after a first UTI is not recommended; it is considered only after recurrent UTIs or significant reflux per RCH CPG: UTI.

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric urology for outpatient cystoscopy and biopsy within the next 6 weeks
options.rationale: Cystoscopy has no role in the initial workup of a first febrile UTI; imaging is non-invasive and is the standard first step per RCH CPG: UTI.

explanation.summary: First febrile UTI in a child under 2 years is followed by renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks. Atypical UTI (non-E. coli, poor response, septic, palpable bladder) prompts earlier imaging and MCUG/DMSA. Routine prophylaxis is not given.
key_points: Ultrasound is first-line after first febrile UTI under 2 years
MCUG reserved for atypical / recurrent UTI or abnormal ultrasound
DMSA at 4-6 months looks for cortical scarring
Educate on perineal hygiene and prompt fever workup
explanation.pearls: A non-E. coli UTI in an infant is an atypical UTI and triggers earlier and more extensive imaging.

stem:
A 3-year-old girl is reviewed two weeks after her second culture-proven E coli febrile UTI (fever to 39 deg C) treated with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole per local sensitivities. The first UTI was 4 months ago. She has ongoing functional constipation well controlled with macrogol. She is otherwise well, growth on the 60th centile, with normal developmental milestones and an unremarkable antenatal renal ultrasound.

There is no family history of renal disease or VUR. Renal ultrasound after the recent admission shows mildly dilated left renal pelvis (AP diameter 8 mm), no hydroureter, no scarring, no duplex system, and normal bladder wall with no post-void residual. The GP is planning the next investigation.

data_table.Vital signs: Afebrile today. HR 100, RR 22, BP 96/60.
data_table.Examination: Well, abdomen soft, no flank tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Renal USS: left renal pelvis AP 8 mm, no hydroureter, no scarring, no duplex. Urine MCS clear since treatment.

lead_in: Which next investigation is most appropriate, and when should it be performed?

options.text: DMSA scan 4-6 months after the most recent UTI to detect cortical scarring; reserve MCUG for hydroureter, abnormal DMSA or breakthrough infections
options.rationale: RCH CPG: UTI and KHA-CARI: after recurrent febrile UTI in a young child, DMSA at 4-6 months post-infection detects persistent scarring (acute inflammation has settled by then). MCUG is invasive and is reserved for hydroureter, abnormal DMSA or breakthrough disease, not first-line. Concurrent constipation management is essential.

options.text: MCUG within the next week as the next investigation, with prophylactic trimethoprim continued until the result is reviewed in clinic
options.rationale: MCUG (catheter, ionising radiation) is not first-line after recurrent UTI in AU pathway; DMSA is preferred to stratify further imaging. Would be correct with hydroureter, abnormal voiding or DMSA-detected scarring (Source: KHA-CARI; RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence trimethoprim nightly as long-term prophylaxis without any further imaging to delineate cortical scarring or vesicoureteric reflux pattern
options.rationale: Prophylaxis is considered after risk stratification (high-grade VUR, significant scarring); empirical prophylaxis without imaging is not first-line. Would be correct after DMSA shows significant scarring or high-grade VUR (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Refer for urological cystoscopy to evaluate the bladder mucosa for inflammation or anatomical abnormality before any other further imaging investigation
options.rationale: Cystoscopy is not indicated in straightforward recurrent UTI without anatomical concerns on US. Would be correct with bladder anomaly on US (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Repeat renal ultrasound in 12 months and otherwise observe without DMSA or MCUG, with watchful waiting and parental return advice for fever
options.rationale: After two febrile UTIs, deferred imaging misses scarring; DMSA at 4-6 months is recommended. Would be correct with single UTI and resolved clinical picture (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: After recurrent febrile UTI in young children, RCH and KHA-CARI recommend renal ultrasound acutely and DMSA 4-6 months later to assess for cortical scarring. MCUG is targeted (abnormal USS, abnormal DMSA, or further breakthroughs). Constipation contributes substantially to UTI recurrence and must be optimised.
key_points: USS during/after first febrile UTI in any child <2 yo
DMSA 4-6 months after recurrent febrile UTI
MCUG if hydroureter, dilated collecting system, or further breakthrough
Treat constipation - reduces UTI recurrence
Prophylaxis individualised: high-grade VUR, severe scarring, breakthrough UTI
explanation.pearls: If you can fix the constipation you fix the UTI in many of these children.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Urinary tract infection

subtopic_detail: Imaging after a second febrile UTI in a young girl

stem:
A 4-month-old, ex-39-week, fully vaccinated, formula-fed girl is brought to a regional ED by her mother after 24 hours of fever to 38.7 degC and increased irritability with poor feeding. There is no cough, coryza, rash, vomiting or diarrhoea. She was born by uncomplicated vaginal birth and discharged on day 2. Antenatal ultrasounds were reported as normal. She has had no previous febrile illnesses and is thriving on the 50th centile for weight.

On arrival she is alert, well perfused, has a flat fontanelle, clear chest and a soft non-tender abdomen with no palpable masses. Vitals: T 38.5 degC, HR 158, RR 38, SpO2 99% RA, cap refill 2 s. The triage nurse asks how to collect a urine sample. The local pathway specifies that any positive urine result must be confirmed on a sample suitable for accurate culture and quantitative colony count (Kass criteria) before committing to a treatment course. The mother is happy to wait. She has no urinary catheter contraindications and no coagulopathy. A bedside ultrasound machine is available.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.5 degC, HR 158, RR 38, SpO2 99% RA, cap refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused, fontanelle flat, chest clear, abdomen soft, no flank mass
data_table.Investigations: Nil sent; awaiting urine collection

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate urine collection method?

options.text: Adhesive perineal bag urine sample for microscopy and culture
options.rationale: Bag specimens have ~60 percent contamination and Kass threshold cannot be applied; a positive bag must be confirmed. Would never be confirmatory; only useful as screening to be confirmed (Source: RCH CPG: UTI).

options.text: In-out urethral catheter specimen for microscopy and culture
options.rationale: Catheter is for unwell infants needing immediate antibiotics or after failed clean catch; invasive and not first-line in well child whose mother can wait. Would be correct only in septic infant or failed clean catch (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Clean catch midstream urine after gentle suprapubic stimulation
options.rationale: In a pre-continent infant the RCH and NICE preferred non-invasive method is clean catch after perineal cleaning, often facilitated by bladder stimulation manoeuvres; growth of greater than 10^5 cfu/mL of a single organism meets Kass criteria for UTI (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection; NICE NG224).

options.text: Suprapubic bladder aspiration performed under bedside ultrasound guidance
options.rationale: SPA is gold standard but invasive/painful and reserved for failed less-invasive collection or immediate sterile sample in septic infant. Would be correct in septic infant when other methods fail (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Cotton wool ball squeezed from nappy onto dipstick
options.rationale: Nappy pad/cotton wool is unreliable for culture (contamination, cell lysis); not recommended for confirmatory specimens. Would never be confirmatory (Source: NICE NG224).

explanation.summary: In a well pre-continent infant requiring confirmatory culture, clean catch is the preferred non-invasive method; bag specimens are inadequate, and SPA or catheter samples are reserved for unwell infants or failed clean catch (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).
key_points: Kass criterion: clean catch growth greater than 10^5 cfu/mL single organism = UTI
Catheter sample threshold: greater than 5 x 10^4 cfu/mL
SPA: any growth of a single Gram-negative organism is significant
Bag specimens used only for screening; positives must be confirmed
Bladder stimulation (suprapubic tap, paravertebral massage) improves clean catch yield
explanation.pearls: If the infant is septic and requires antibiotics within the hour, catheter or SPA is appropriate so cultures are not delayed.

sources.label: AAP - UTI in febrile infants 2-24 months

stem:
An 8-week-old, ex-38-week, breastfed boy is brought to a metropolitan ED by his parents with 18 hours of fever to 38.6 degC, increased fussiness and reduced feeding. There is no cough, rash, vomiting or diarrhoea. His birth was uncomplicated; maternal GBS was negative; he received vitamin K and hepatitis B at birth. Routine antenatal ultrasounds showed no renal tract abnormality.

He is on the 25th centile for weight and is thriving. On arrival he is alert, settles to feed but tires quickly. Examination: T 38.4 degC, HR 162, RR 48, SpO2 99 percent RA, cap refill 2 s, fontanelle flat, no rash, chest clear, abdomen soft, circumcision recent and clean. A catheter urine specimen shows leucocyte esterase positive, nitrites positive, microscopy 200 WBC per high power field with Gram-negative rods.

Bloods: WCC 14.2 x10^9/L, neutrophils 8.4, CRP 42 mg/L, venous lactate 1.2 mmol/L, glucose 4.6 mmol/L, sodium 137. The ED registrar has commenced IV access and given a 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4 degC, HR 162, RR 48, SpO2 99% RA, cap refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Alert, tires with feeds, fontanelle flat, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Catheter urine: LE+, nitrites+, 200 WBC/HPF, GNR. WCC 14.2, CRP 42, lactate 1.2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge home on oral trimethoprim with GP review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Outpatient oral is not appropriate in under-3-months with culture-suggestive UTI (high bacteraemia/meningitis risk). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: UTI).

options.text: Admit for nasogastric fluids and observation without antibiotics pending culture
options.rationale: Antibiotics must not be withheld pending formal culture once urine microscopy is positive in this age group. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Short-stay observation on oral cefalexin and discharge if afebrile at 6 hours
options.rationale: Short stay with oral does not meet under-3-months pathway; IV plus LP are mandated regardless of clinical appearance. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Admit for IV ceftriaxone monotherapy without performing a lumbar puncture today
options.rationale: LP cannot be deferred in under-3-months with proven UTI (bacteraemia/meningitis risk), and ceftriaxone monotherapy omits Listeria cover. Would be correct only in over-3-months with selective workup (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Admit for IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin and perform a lumbar puncture
options.rationale: Under 3 months with a positive urine result requires admission, blood culture, lumbar puncture and IV cover against E. coli, GBS and Listeria; benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin is the RCH and eTG empirical regimen (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection; Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Infants under 3 months with a positive urine require admission, blood culture, LP and IV penicillin plus gentamicin to cover E. coli, GBS and Listeria; oral therapy and outpatient management are unsafe (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).
key_points: Under 3 months UTI = admit, septic workup, IV antibiotics
Empirical regimen: IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin
LP recommended given bacteraemia rate up to 10 percent and meningitis up to 2 percent
Switch to oral after 48 hours if culture sensitive and child improving
Total course typically 7-10 days
explanation.pearls: A positive urine in a young infant is not just a UTI; treat as occult bacteraemia until cultures return.

stem:
A 3-year-old girl presents to her GP for review after a third culture-confirmed E. coli UTI in 12 months. The first episode at 18 months was treated with oral trimethoprim with rapid recovery; the second at 2 years required a 48-hour admission with IV gentamicin for fever, vomiting and a CRP of 110 mg/L. The current episode resolved after 5 days of oral cefalexin.

She is now well, afebrile, growing along the 50th centile and is fully toilet trained without daytime wetting. She has no constipation, no abnormal voiding patternc reflux and no antenatal renal abnormality on the 20-week scan. A renal tract ultrasound performed two days ago showed normal kidney size with no hydronephrosis, normal corticomedullary differentiation and complete bladder emptying.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/58 mmHg, afebrile
data_table.Examination: Normal genitalia, no flank tenderness, no palpable bladder
data_table.Investigations: Urea 4.2, creatinine 32, eGFR normal. Renal USS normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Repeat renal tract ultrasound in 6 months and continue clinical follow up
options.rationale: US is poor for detecting cortical scarring and low-grade VUR, both of which change long-term management after recurrent/atypical UTI. Would be correct only as initial screen (Source: NICE NG224).

options.text: DMSA renal scan in 4-6 months to assess for cortical scarring
options.rationale: In a continent child over 3 years with recurrent or atypical UTI, DMSA is preferred over MCUG to detect renal cortical scarring and guide nephrology follow up; it is delayed 4-6 months after the acute infection to avoid false positives from acute pyelonephritis (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection; NICE NG224).

options.text: Micturating cystourethrogram with fluoroscopy to grade vesicoureteric reflux severity
options.rationale: MCUG is invasive (catheterisation, contrast); reserved for under-6-months, atypical/recurrent UTI with abnormal US, or family history of VUR. Would be correct only in those specific groups (Source: KHA-CARI).

options.text: MAG3 diuretic renogram to assess differential function and obstructive drainage
options.rationale: MAG3 evaluates differential function and drainage in suspected obstruction; her US shows no hydronephrosis. Would be correct only with hydronephrosis/obstruction suspicion (Source: NICE NG224).

options.text: Non-contrast CT urogram to evaluate the renal cortex and collecting system
options.rationale: CT delivers high radiation and is not first-line for scarring in children; DMSA gives functional cortical information with lower radiation. Would never be correct first-line (Source: RANZCR).

explanation.summary: After recurrent UTI in a child over 3 years with normal ultrasound, DMSA at 4-6 months post-infection assesses cortical scarring; MCUG is reserved for selected high-risk groups, and CT is avoided due to radiation (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection; NICE NG224).
key_points: Ultrasound for all under 6 months and any atypical or recurrent UTI
DMSA detects cortical scarring; perform 4-6 months after acute infection
MCUG indicated under 6 months, atypical UTI, or abnormal USS/DMSA or family history of VUR
Recurrent = 2+ upper-tract or 3+ lower-tract UTIs
Antibiotic prophylaxis only after imaging suggests VUR or scarring
explanation.pearls: Avoid early DMSA - acute pyelonephritis causes reversible cold spots that look like scars.

stem:
A 9-month-old fully vaccinated boy is brought to his GP with 36 hours of low-grade fever (38.1 deg C) and mild irritability. He has had no rash, vomiting, diarrhoea or cough. He has 5-6 wet nappies a day and is feeding only slightly less than usual. He is otherwise well, on the 50th centile and developmentally normal. There were no antenatal renal concerns.

He is uncircumcised. Examination: alert, well perfused, flat fontanelle, clear chest, soft non-tender abdomen, no palpable bladder, no flank tenderness. T 37.9, HR 132, RR 28, SpO2 99 percent. The practice nurse applied an adhesive perineal bag for screening urinalysis. Bag urine: LE trace, nitrites negative, blood negative, protein negative, microscopy 8 WBC per high power field with no organisms seen. The lab offers same-day clean-catch processing.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.9 degC, HR 132, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused, abdomen soft, no flank tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Bag urine: LE trace, nitrites neg, blood neg, protein neg, 8 WBC/HPF

lead_in: How should the bag urine result be interpreted and what is the next step?

options.text: Bag urine has poor specificity but the negative result reliably excludes UTI in this clinical context; discharge with safety-net advice for parents
options.rationale: Bag urine cannot reliably exclude UTI: nitrites require 4+ h bladder dwell (often negative in frequent voiders) and not all uropathogens reduce nitrates. Would never be correct (Source: NICE NG224; RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence empirical oral trimethoprim while awaiting a repeat bag specimen for culture confirmation over the next 48 hours of observation
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics should not be started on bag urine alone; reliable confirmatory sample is required first. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Negative nitrites reliably rule out E. coli UTI in this child given the typical urinary dwell time before bag collection in nappies
options.rationale: Negative nitrites do not exclude UTI in infants (short bladder dwell, non-nitrate-reducing uropathogens). Would never be correct (Source: NICE NG224).
source_refs: AAP - UTI in febrile infants 2-24 months

options.text: Send the bag specimen for culture; a negative culture result will definitively exclude UTI without further sterile sampling required
options.rationale: Bag samples have ~60 percent contamination and do not meet Kass criteria; bag-urine culture cannot reliably exclude infection. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Bag urine is unreliable for diagnosing or excluding UTI; if suspicion remains, obtain clean catch (catheter if unwell); do not start antibiotics on bag urine
options.rationale: Bag-urine-as-diagnostic is the anti-pattern. Australian guidance (RCH CPG: UTI, NICE NG224): bag urine is for screening only because of high contamination and unreliable nitrites; a clean-catch (or catheter/SPA if unwell) is required for diagnosis. Antibiotics should not be started on bag urine alone.

explanation.summary: Bag urine is a screening test only and cannot exclude or confirm UTI; clinical suspicion in a febrile pre-continent child requires clean catch or catheter sampling for diagnosis (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).
key_points: Bag contamination rate ~60 percent
Nitrites need >=4 hour bladder dwell to be reliable
Not all uropathogens reduce nitrate (eg Enterococcus, Pseudomonas)
Confirm any abnormal bag dip with clean catch or catheter sample
Symptoms + sterile-method culture, not bag urine, drive treatment
explanation.pearls: Trust the clinical picture more than the bag urine - a well child with negative bag and no fever may still need watchful waiting; a febrile child needs a reliable sample.

sources.label: AAP - UTI in febrile infants 2-24 months

stem:
A primigravida attends a postnatal ward review at 8 hours after an uncomplicated term vaginal birth. The 32-week growth scan reported isolated SFU grade 2 left hydronephrosis with renal pelvis anteroposterior diameter 8 mm, normal contralateral kidney, normal ureters, normal bladder, normal amniotic fluid index and no other anomalies. The male infant (ex-40-week) is breastfeeding well, alert, well perfused with normal vitals.

Examination: normal cardiorespiratory examination, no flank masses, bilateral descended testes, normal penis with glanular meatus, no spinal abnormality, normal hip examination, and a documented strong urinary stream. No family history of renal disease, VUR or consanguinity. The hospital offers outpatient renal ultrasound from day of life 4 onwards. Discharge home is planned for tomorrow.

data_table.Examination: Normal newborn, no flank masses, normal genitalia, strong urinary stream observed
data_table.Antenatal: SFU grade 2 left hydronephrosis, AP diameter 8 mm at 32 weeks, normal ureters/bladder/AFI

lead_in: When should the postnatal renal tract ultrasound be performed, and what additional imaging (if any) is needed at this stage?

options.text: Within the first 24 hours of life before discharge from the postnatal ward, plus a routine MCUG before discharge regardless of severity grade
options.rationale: Imaging in the first 48 h underestimates hydronephrosis (physiological neonatal oliguria); routine MCUG not indicated for isolated SFU 1-2. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCR; RCH CPG).

options.text: Within the first 12 hours of life with a concurrent MCUG before any postnatal feeds or planned discharge from the postnatal ward
options.rationale: Early scanning and routine MCUG are both inappropriate for isolated mild-moderate antenatal hydronephrosis. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCR).

options.text: At 3 months of age routinely regardless of severity grade, without any earlier postnatal imaging or paediatric urology specialist input
options.rationale: Three-month deferral risks missing significant uropathy; SFU 2 should be assessed by 6 weeks. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Only if the infant develops a febrile illness or fails to thrive; with no routine postnatal imaging otherwise, regardless of grade
options.rationale: Symptom-driven imaging misses asymptomatic obstructive uropathy and VUR; all antenatally detected hydronephrosis needs postnatal confirmation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Renal tract ultrasound between day 4 and 6 weeks of life as an outpatient; MCUG NOT required for isolated SFU 1-2; reserve MCUG for SFU 3-4 or suspected PUV
options.rationale: RCH CPG: Antenatal hydronephrosis recommends postnatal renal ultrasound after day 4 once neonatal oliguria resolves; for isolated SFU 1-2 by 6 weeks is appropriate. SFU 3-4, bilateral disease, or suspected PUV warrant earlier imaging in the first days of life, and MCUG is reserved for higher-grade or abnormal findings.
source_refs: Society for Foetal Urology - Hydronephrosis grading

explanation.summary: Postnatal ultrasound for isolated mild/moderate antenatal hydronephrosis is performed after day 4 (and by 6 weeks) to avoid false reassurance from neonatal oliguria; severe or bilateral disease or suspected PUV requires earlier imaging (RCH CPG - Antenatal hydronephrosis).
key_points: SFU grading: 1 (pelvis only), 2 (calyces), 3 (calyceal dilatation), 4 (parenchymal thinning)
Mild/moderate isolated: USS days 4 to 6 weeks
Severe/bilateral/PUV-suspected: USS day 1-2 + paeds urology referral
Persistent hydronephrosis: MAG3 for obstruction, MCUG for VUR
Trimethoprim prophylaxis usually only for high-grade or VUR-confirmed disease
explanation.pearls: An infant with isolated mild-moderate antenatal hydronephrosis and normal newborn exam can often be discharged with an outpatient scan and surgical review.

sources.label: Society for Foetal Urology - Hydronephrosis grading

stem: A 9-month-old boy presents with 6 days of fever up to 39.5 degrees Celsius that has not responded to paracetamol or ibuprofen. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, cracked erythematous lips, and irritability. He does not have a rash, cervical lymphadenopathy, or limb changes. All cultures and viral studies are negative. Urinalysis shows sterile pyuria.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 165, RR 36, SpO2 98% RA, Temp 39.3
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WCC 18.2 x10^9/L, Hb 98 g/L, platelets 480 x10^9/L; CRP 125 mg/L; ESR 72 mm/hr; ALT 65 U/L; albumin 26 g/L; urinalysis: WCC 15/hpf, no growth on culture; blood cultures negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue supportive care and reassess in 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: This child has 6 days of fever with 2 of 5 classic Kawasaki criteria (conjunctival injection, lip changes), meeting the definition of incomplete Kawasaki disease. Infants under 12 months are at highest risk of coronary artery aneurysms and incomplete presentations are more common in this age group. Delaying treatment increases the risk of coronary involvement. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence high-dose aspirin and arrange outpatient echocardiography
options.rationale: Aspirin is part of Kawasaki management but is given alongside IVIG, not alone. Furthermore, echocardiography should be performed urgently (not as an outpatient) given the 6 days of fever and high risk of coronary involvement in an infant. Aspirin monotherapy is insufficient to prevent coronary artery aneurysms. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Request urgent echocardiography and administer IVIG 2 g/kg
options.rationale: This infant meets criteria for incomplete Kawasaki disease: fever for 5 or more days with 2-3 classic features, plus supportive laboratory findings (CRP >30, ESR >40, albumin <30, anaemia, thrombocytosis, elevated ALT, sterile pyuria). In infants under 12 months with incomplete Kawasaki, the threshold for treatment should be low due to higher coronary risk. IVIG 2 g/kg over 10-12 hours plus high-dose aspirin is the standard treatment. Echocardiography assesses coronary arteries. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Start empiric IV antibiotics for possible occult bacterial focus
options.rationale: Blood and urine cultures are negative, and the sterile pyuria is characteristic of Kawasaki disease rather than urinary tract infection. The clinical picture with prolonged fever, conjunctival injection, lip changes, and supportive laboratories is more consistent with Kawasaki disease than occult bacterial infection. Antibiotics would not prevent coronary artery aneurysms. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Obtain a CT angiogram of the coronary arteries for assessment
options.rationale: CT angiography is not the initial cardiac investigation in suspected Kawasaki disease. Transthoracic echocardiography is the standard imaging modality for assessing coronary artery dimensions in children. CT carries radiation exposure and typically requires sedation. It may be used later for detailed follow-up of known aneurysms. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is defined by fever for 5 or more days with fewer than 4 of the 5 classic clinical criteria, supported by laboratory abnormalities. It is more common in infants under 12 months, who are paradoxically at highest risk of coronary artery aneurysms. The laboratory features supporting the diagnosis include CRP above 30 mg/L, ESR above 40 mm/hr, hypoalbuminaemia, anaemia, thrombocytosis, elevated transaminases, and sterile pyuria. Treatment with IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin should not be delayed.
key_points: Incomplete Kawasaki: fever >=5 days with 2-3 classic criteria plus supportive lab findings
Infants under 12 months have the highest risk of coronary aneurysms and most commonly present incompletely
IVIG 2 g/kg as a single infusion over 10-12 hours is the standard treatment
Sterile pyuria is a supportive laboratory finding for Kawasaki disease
Treatment within 10 days of fever onset reduces coronary aneurysm risk from 25% to approximately 4%
explanation.pearls: Sterile pyuria in Kawasaki disease results from urethral inflammation causing white cells in urine that is culture-negative - it occurs in up to 80% of cases and should not be mistaken for UTI.

stem: A 14-month-old boy has just completed a 7-day course of cephalexin for his first culture-confirmed febrile urinary tract infection caused by Escherichia coli sensitive to amoxicillin and cephalexin. He defervesced within 48 hours of antibiotics. Antenatal scans were normal. He is now well, growing along the 50th centile, voiding normally and feeding well. Mother asks about follow-up imaging because his older brother had a renal scan.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, RR 28, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing, abdomen soft and non-tender, no flank tenderness, external genitalia normal
data_table.Investigations: Urine culture pre-treatment: E. coli, sensitive to amoxicillin and cephalexin. Post-treatment urine culture not done.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate follow-up imaging plan?

options.text: Renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks; escalate only if abnormal
options.rationale: Routine renal tract ultrasound is not required after a first typical febrile UTI per the Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG. Ultrasound during the acute illness is recommended for atypical UTI (poor response, non-E coli organism, sepsis, abdominal mass, raised creatinine) and for infants under 6 months with recurrent UTI or no antenatal monitoring. This child had a typical UTI, defervesced in 48 hours, has normal antenatal scans, and is over 6 months old - imaging is not indicated. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

options.text: Micturating cystourethrogram now and DMSA scan at 6 months
options.rationale: MCUG and DMSA are reserved for recurrent UTI, atypical features, or abnormal renal tract ultrasound; both involve radiation and MCUG is invasive. Neither is indicated after a single typical UTI. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG: UTI)

options.text: DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months to assess for renal scarring
options.rationale: DMSA is reserved for atypical or recurrent UTI, abnormal ultrasound or high suspicion of reflux nephropathy; a single typical UTI with good response does not require it. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG: UTI)

options.text: Daily trimethoprim prophylaxis with no further imaging arranged
options.rationale: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis is not recommended after a single uncomplicated febrile UTI in an otherwise well child. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG: UTI)

options.text: No additional imaging is required; counsel parents on prompt presentation for any future fever and arrange GP follow-up
options.rationale: Per the Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG, no follow-up imaging is required after a first typical febrile UTI in a well child over 6 months with normal antenatal scans and good response to first-line antibiotics. Renal tract ultrasound is reserved for atypical UTI (poor response, non-E coli, sepsis, abdominal mass, raised creatinine), recurrent UTI, or infants under 6 months with recurrent UTI or absent antenatal monitoring. Counsel parents on prompt presentation for any future fever and arrange GP follow-up. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

explanation.summary: After a first typical febrile UTI in a child over 6 months with normal antenatal scans and good response to first-line antibiotics, the Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG does NOT recommend routine imaging. Imaging is triggered by ATYPICAL features (slow response, non-E coli organism, sepsis or septicaemia, abdominal mass, raised creatinine) or by RECURRENCE. Infants under 6 months get ultrasound if recurrent UTI or no antenatal monitoring. DMSA and MCUG are reserved for abnormal ultrasound, recurrent infection, suspected posterior urethral valves or marked hydronephrosis.
key_points: First typical febrile UTI in a well child over 6 months with normal antenatal scans - no routine imaging (RCH Melbourne CPG)
Ultrasound triggered by atypical UTI or recurrence, or by infants under 6 months with recurrent UTI or no antenatal scans
DMSA and MCUG are reserved for abnormal ultrasound, recurrent UTI, or suspected PUV
Counsel parents to present early for any future fever
explanation.pearls: Atypical UTI features per RCH Melbourne are: poor response to 48 hours of antibiotics, non-E coli organism, septicaemia, abdominal or bladder mass, raised creatinine, or recurrence - any one triggers ultrasound.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 7-week-old previously well term male infant brought in by parents with 24 hours of fever, irritability, and reduced feeding (taking about half his usual volume). He has had no vomiting or diarrhoea. There is no rash, no recent travel, and immunisations are up to date. ED has obtained a catheter urine specimen which is positive for nitrites and leukocyte esterase. Bloods are back.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7, HR 178, RR 52, SpO2 98% RA, cap refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Alert when held; wet nappies reduced; no rash; chest clear; abdomen soft, no mass; suprapubic non-tender
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.4, neutrophils 13.2, Hb 110, CRP 84, Na 138, K 4.6, Cr 28; urine: nitrites +, leuk +++, blood +, microscopy WCC >100/mcL; blood and urine cultures sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days with GP review within 48 hours arranged
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics and outpatient management are inappropriate for febrile UTI under 3 months of age; the bacteraemia risk is too high. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Admit for IV gentamicin and IV benzylpenicillin with renal tract ultrasound during admission
options.rationale: Infants under 3 months with febrile UTI require admission and parenteral antibiotics; empirical IV gentamicin plus benzylpenicillin (or amoxicillin) is standard, and renal tract ultrasound is recommended during first febrile UTI in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily and arrange micturating cystourethrogram during this admission
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is reserved for late-onset neonatal sepsis or proven resistant organisms; MCUG is not first-line imaging after a first febrile UTI under current Australian practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg three times daily with paediatric review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Oral antibiotic monotherapy is not acceptable for febrile UTI under 3 months irrespective of agent due to bacteraemia and meningitis risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Discharge home awaiting urine culture sensitivities and start antibiotics if a pathogen grows
options.rationale: Awaiting cultures before treating any febrile infant under 3 months with positive urine dipstick risks progression to urosepsis and meningitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Febrile UTI in infants under 3 months is treated as serious bacterial infection until proven otherwise: admission, parenteral antibiotics, blood and urine cultures, and consideration of lumbar puncture if clinically unwell or under 4 weeks. Empirical IV gentamicin plus benzylpenicillin (or amoxicillin) is the standard Australian regimen, with oral switch when stable and afebrile typically after 2 to 4 days. Renal tract ultrasound is recommended after a first febrile UTI under 6 months to identify obstruction or significant abnormality. MCUG is reserved for recurrent UTI, atypical organism, or abnormal ultrasound. DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months assesses cortical scarring when indicated. E. coli accounts for over 80% of paediatric UTIs.
key_points: Under 3 months febrile UTI: admit, IV antibiotics, blood + urine cultures
Empirical IV gentamicin + benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin
Renal tract ultrasound after first febrile UTI under 6 months
MCUG only if recurrent, atypical, or abnormal USS
Catheter or SPA urine specimen preferred under 2 years
explanation.pearls: Gentamicin once-daily at 7.5 mg/kg in neonates and infants is preferred to divided dosing in Australian paediatric practice because of superior peak-to-MIC bactericidal activity and reduced nephrotoxicity.

subtopic_detail: Urinary tract infection empirical antibiotic in a young child
stem: You are the emergency department registrar assessing a 14-month-old girl with 2 days of fever and irritability. She is feeding poorly but is alert and tracking carers. There is no cough, coryza or rash. She has no allergies and has had no recent antibiotics. A clean-catch urine sample has been collected. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.2 °C, HR 158, RR 32, SpO2 99% room air, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused, clear chest, soft abdomen, no rash, ears and throat normal
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: leucocytes 3+, nitrites positive, blood trace, protein 1+. Urine microscopy and culture pending.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical management while awaiting urine culture?

options.text: Start oral trimethoprim plus sulfamethoxazole at standard paediatric dosing for 7 days and arrange follow-up with the GP at the end of the course
options.rationale: Trimethoprim with sulfamethoxazole is first-line empirical oral therapy for uncomplicated paediatric urinary tract infection in Australia, with a 7-day course in children under 2 years; she is well enough for oral therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit for intravenous gentamicin and benzylpenicillin pending the urine culture result and reassess for oral switch at 48 hours of treatment
options.rationale: Intravenous therapy is reserved for infants under 3 months, suspected pyelonephritis with sepsis, vomiting or inability to tolerate oral antibiotics; this child is well perfused and tolerating oral intake. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until urine culture confirms a uropathogen growth above 10^8 colony-forming units per litre to avoid driving resistance
options.rationale: Delaying treatment while waiting for a culture result risks progression to pyelonephritis or sepsis and is not consistent with Australian paediatric urinary tract infection management. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

options.text: Start oral amoxicillin alone at standard paediatric dosing for 5 days with planned follow-up at the GP at the end of the antibiotic course
options.rationale: Amoxicillin alone has unacceptably high resistance among community Escherichia coli isolates in Australia and is not first-line empirical therapy for paediatric urinary tract infection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Reassure the family and arrange a repeat urine specimen at the GP in 48 hours with antibiotics deferred until microscopy returns positive findings
options.rationale: A positive nitrite and leucocyte dipstick in a febrile child under 2 years is sufficient to start empirical antibiotics; deferring treatment risks progression. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

explanation.summary: Febrile urinary tract infection in young children is treated empirically once a clean-catch urine sample has been collected, because untreated infection risks renal scarring. Oral trimethoprim with sulfamethoxazole or cefalexin are the Australian first-line agents for clinically uncomplicated cases; intravenous therapy is reserved for infants under 3 months, sepsis or vomiting. A urine culture is sent to refine therapy at 48 hours and to identify resistant organisms. Imaging is indicated after a first febrile urinary tract infection in this age group.
key_points: Trimethoprim with sulfamethoxazole is first-line oral empirical therapy in Australian children over 1 month
Amoxicillin alone is not recommended because of high Escherichia coli resistance rates
Intravenous antibiotics are reserved for infants under 3 months, sepsis or oral intolerance
Renal tract ultrasound is recommended after a first febrile urinary tract infection in children under 2 years
Treatment duration is 7 days in children under 2 and may be 3 days in older children with cystitis
explanation.pearls: Recurrent febrile urinary tract infections or abnormal initial ultrasound trigger a micturating cystourethrogram or dimercaptosuccinic acid scan to look for vesicoureteric reflux and renal scarring.

stem: You are the paediatric ED registrar reviewing a 5-week-old term infant brought in by his parents with a documented temperature of 38.4 at home and irritability over 6 hours. He is alert in the cot, feeding well, with no respiratory or skin findings. His parents recall a recent viral illness in an older sibling. He has no risk factors for invasive group B Streptococcus and was discharged well at day 3.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.5, HR 168, RR 48, SpO2 98% RA, BP 78/44; capillary glucose 4.6
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused; anterior fontanelle soft; clear chest; soft abdomen; no rash; no focal source
data_table.Investigations: FBC WCC 14.2 (neutrophils 9.2), Hb 132, plt 320; CRP 32; urine collected by SPA pending; blood culture pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with paracetamol 15 mg/kg orally as required and general practitioner review in 24 hours; safety-net advice
options.rationale: Infants under 3 months with temperature 38 or above require admission and full septic workup regardless of clinical appearance; outpatient management is not standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

options.text: Admit; observe without antibiotics or lumbar puncture pending 24-hour blood and urine culture results
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are mandatory in febrile infants under 3 months pending culture results, regardless of clinical wellness, because of bacteraemia and meningitis risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

options.text: Admit; commence intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg; defer lumbar puncture pending repeat clinical assessment in 6 hours
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin monotherapy does not cover Listeria or Escherichia coli, the dominant causes in this age group; broader empirical cover is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit; perform lumbar puncture, commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg, gentamicin 5 mg/kg and cefotaxime 50 mg/kg
options.rationale: Febrile infant 1 to 3 months requires full septic workup including lumbar puncture and immediate empirical cover for invasive bacterial infection including Listeria, group B Streptococcus and gram negatives. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Perform urine dipstick on bag urine only; commence oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily and discharge home with review
options.rationale: Bag-collected urine is unreliable for diagnosis under 12 months due to contamination; oral antibiotics are inadequate for suspected invasive sepsis in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Febrile infants under 3 months have a higher risk of occult invasive bacterial infection including meningitis even when clinically well-appearing. Standard Australian practice is to admit, obtain blood, urine (suprapubic aspirate or catheter) and CSF cultures, and start empirical antibiotics covering Listeria monocytogenes (benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin), group B Streptococcus and gram-negative organisms (gentamicin plus a third-generation cephalosporin). De-escalation occurs at 36 to 48 hours based on cultures and clinical course.
key_points: Febrile infants under 3 months: full septic workup including LP
Empirical antibiotics cover Listeria, GBS and gram-negatives
Bag urine is unreliable below 12 months
De-escalate at 36 to 48 hours based on cultures
explanation.pearls: Cefotaxime is preferred over ceftriaxone in neonates under 28 days due to risk of bilirubin displacement and biliary sludge; switch to ceftriaxone after 28 days.

subtopic_detail: Empiric IV antibiotic regimen in febrile infant under 3 months
stem: A 2-month-old infant is brought to your ED with 24 hours of fever (38.6 by parents), poor feeding, irritability, and a single episode of vomiting. He has no upper respiratory or other localising symptoms. He is fully immunised for age. On examination he is alert but irritable, with a temperature of 38.7, capillary refill 2 seconds, no rash, no meningism, and clear chest. His urine is collected by clean catch.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7, HR 165, RR 50, SpO2 99 percent RA, weight 5.2 kg
data_table.Examination: Alert but irritable; no rash; anterior fontanelle soft; chest clear; no abdominal tenderness; perfusion adequate
data_table.Investigations: Bedside urine dipstick positive for leukocytes and nitrites; FBC, CRP, blood cultures, blood gas pending; LP being arranged

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic management?

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin empiric coverage following blood and urine cultures and lumbar puncture
options.rationale: In infants under 3 months with fever, the threshold for serious bacterial infection workup and empiric antibiotic cover is low; sepsis cannot be excluded clinically. The RCH approach is full septic screen (blood culture, urine catheter or SPA, LP) and empiric IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin, covering E. coli, Listeria, Group B strep, and other neonatal pathogens. (Source: RCH CPG; eTG)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg/dose four times daily for 7 days as outpatient management with GP follow-up
options.rationale: Oral therapy is inappropriate in febrile infants under 3 months; sepsis evaluation and IV therapy are mandatory. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone monotherapy at 50 mg/kg once daily without need for second agent
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone alone covers most enterics but does not reliably cover Listeria, which is important in this age group; AU empiric regimen includes benzylpenicillin or ampicillin for Listeria. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole orally as suitable empiric treatment for outpatient management of UTI
options.rationale: Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is inappropriate for septic febrile infant; risks delayed source control and bilirubin displacement in neonates. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until urine culture is back at 48 hours and reassess clinically before commencing therapy
options.rationale: Withholding antibiotics in a febrile young infant is dangerous; outcomes worsen markedly with each hour of delay. (Source: RCH CPG)

explanation.summary: Fever in infants under 3 months requires systematic evaluation because clinical signs are unreliable for identifying serious bacterial infection (SBI, around 10 percent prevalence). AU practice: full septic screen (blood cultures, urine via catheter or suprapubic aspirate, LP) and empiric IV antibiotics. Standard regimen IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin (or amoxicillin plus gentamicin) covers E. coli, Group B Streptococcus, Listeria monocytogenes, and Streptococcus pneumoniae. Add aciclovir if HSV suspected. Admit for at least 48 hours pending culture results.
key_points: Fever under 3 months - full septic screen plus empiric IV antibiotics
Empiric: benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin (covers Listeria plus neonatal pathogens)
Add aciclovir if HSV concern (vesicles, seizures, hepatitis)
Admit minimum 48 hours
explanation.pearls: Suprapubic aspirate is the gold standard urine collection in infants under 6 months when clean-catch is not feasible because bag samples have around a 25 percent contamination rate; ultrasound-guided SPA increases first-pass success to around 95 percent.

stem: A 7-week-old previously healthy girl presents with fever and reduced feeding for 10 hours. She was born at term by spontaneous vaginal delivery and required no neonatal admission. Her mother reports increased sleepiness and only two wet nappies since morning. There is no cough, rhinorrhoea, vomiting, or diarrhoea. On examination the infant briefly opens her eyes to stimulation but settles quickly back to sleep. Peripheral perfusion is mildly delayed. Urinalysis obtained by catheter specimen shows positive leukocyte esterase and nitrites.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.4 C, HR 178 bpm, RR 44/min, BP 82/48 mmHg, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Mild lethargy, delayed capillary refill 3 seconds, soft fontanelle, no increased work of breathing
data_table.Investigations: WCC 17.8 x10^9/L, CRP 42 mg/L, urinalysis positive nitrites and leukocyte esterase

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge with oral cephalexin because a urinary source is the likely cause of fever
options.rationale: Young febrile infants under 3 months require inpatient assessment and parenteral antibiotics due to high invasive bacterial infection risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child).

options.text: Commence empiric intravenous antibiotics and admit for full septic evaluation and observation
options.rationale: This infant is under 3 months with fever, lethargy, inflammatory marker elevation, and likely UTI. Full sepsis assessment and inpatient IV antibiotics are indicated. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile Child)

options.text: Observe in the emergency department without antibiotics until blood culture results become available
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics in a potentially septic young infant is unsafe (rapid deterioration risk). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child

options.text: Arrange renal ultrasound before treatment because structural urinary abnormalities are likely
options.rationale: Renal imaging may be considered later but should not delay urgent evaluation and treatment of febrile UTI in a young infant. Would be correct as adjunct after stabilisation (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Repeat observations after paracetamol because fever alone may explain the tachycardia and lethargy
options.rationale: Fever contributes to tachycardia but lethargy and poor perfusion in a young infant require immediate sepsis-focused management. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child

explanation.summary: Febrile infants younger than 3 months require a low threshold for full septic workup and inpatient management. This infant has lethargy, inflammatory marker elevation, and likely UTI, making IV antibiotics and admission essential. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile Child)
key_points: Young infants may deteriorate rapidly despite initially subtle examination findings. (Source: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child)
Positive nitrites on catheter urine strongly support bacterial UTI. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)
explanation.pearls: A well-appearing moment while being comforted does not reliably exclude serious illness in young infants. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile Child)

sources.label: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child

stem: A 3-month-old female presents to ED with fever (38.6 deg C), irritability and poor feeding for 24 hours. She has no cough, rash or focal infection. Bag urine on the ward showed nitrites positive, leukocyte esterase 3+ and white cells over 100 x 10^6/L. The on-call paediatric registrar is reviewing the next step. The infant is irritable, has a normal fontanelle, and HR 170, RR 44, SpO2 97 percent on room air.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.6°C, HR 170 bpm, RR 44, SpO₂ 97%
data_table.Examination: Irritable, no focus of infection, normal fontanelle
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: nitrites+, LE 3+, WBC >100 ×10⁶/L

lead_in: Which combination of confirmatory sampling, empirical therapy and imaging is most appropriate?

options.text: Commence oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole based on the bag urine and arrange outpatient renal ultrasound within the next 6 weeks of presentation
options.rationale: Bag urine has high contamination rates and is screening only; under-3-months requires hospital admission for parenteral antibiotics with sterile sample. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: UTI).

options.text: Catheter (or SPA) urine for culture before antibiotics; admit and commence IV benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) plus IV gentamicin; arrange renal ultrasound during admission
options.rationale: RCH CPG: UTI requires a STERILE specimen (catheter or suprapubic aspirate) before treatment when bag urine is suggestive, because bag urine can over-diagnose and contaminate cultures. For febrile UTI in an infant under 3 months, admission with IV ampicillin plus gentamicin is recommended (avoiding ceftriaxone in neonates), with renal tract ultrasound during admission to detect structural abnormality; DMSA or MCUG are considered later based on results.

options.text: Trust the bag urine and commence IV antibiotics immediately without obtaining a sterile catheter or SPA specimen first in this febrile neonate
options.rationale: Bag urine has too high false-positive rate to direct treatment; sterile sampling required. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Treat with antipyretics and review in 24 hours, attributing the febrile illness to a presumed viral cause
options.rationale: Urinalysis strongly suggests UTI; untreated infant UTI risks pyelonephritis and scarring. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture before any antibiotics in this febrile neonate as the first procedural step
options.rationale: LP not routinely required for isolated UTI in well-appearing 3-month-old; considered if sepsis/meningitis suspected. Would be correct only with sepsis features (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Urinary tract infection in infants under 3 months presents non-specifically with fever, irritability, poor feeding, and sometimes jaundice or sepsis; localising signs are usually absent. Bag urine is a screening test only - false positive rates exceed 80% due to skin contamination - so a positive bag must be confirmed with a clean catheter specimen or suprapubic aspirate before antibiotics. Suprapubic aspirate is gold standard but ED-dependent. Empirical Australian therapy is IV benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) plus gentamicin pending culture; switch to oral on susceptibility once afebrile 48 hours. All infants under 6 months with first UTI need a renal tract ultrasound within 48 hours (or sooner if atypical or severe). DMSA scan at 4-6 months identifies cortical scarring; MCUG is reserved for recurrent UTI or atypical course. Constipation, dysfunctional voiding and family vesicoureteric reflux are common contributors.
key_points: Infants <3 months with febrile UTI: admit, IV antibiotics, renal ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)
Ampicillin + gentamicin is a typical empiric regimen. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)
explanation.pearls: In a febrile infant under 3 months, bag urine is for screening only - confirmation requires catheter or suprapubic aspirate before antibiotics. Empirical cover is IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin; renal tract ultrasound within 48 hours (RCH CPG UTI).

stem:
A previously well 5-month-old girl is brought to a regional emergency department by her parents with 24 hours of fever to 39 and one episode of vomiting. She is feeding 70% of normal and has decreased wet nappies overnight. She has no rash, no cough, no diarrhoea, no obvious focus on examination, and her ears, throat and chest are clear.

She has not received antibiotics recently. A clean catch urine collected by the midwife shows leucocyte esterase 3+ and nitrites 2+ on dipstick; microscopy shows pyuria. Blood cultures and urine culture have been sent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158, RR 42, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 39.1
data_table.Examination: Alert but irritable; no rash; soft abdomen; clear chest; ears normal
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick LE 3+ nitrites 2+; microscopy pyuria and gram-negative rods; WCC 19; CRP 75; blood culture pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg per kg twice daily for 5 days with general practitioner review in 48 hours after discharge
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with febrile UTI require intravenous antibiotic initiation in Australia until clinically stable and afebrile; oral monotherapy is unsafe at this age. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

options.text: Admit for intravenous gentamicin and intravenous benzylpenicillin or ampicillin pending sensitivities and clinical response
options.rationale: Febrile UTI in infants under 6 months requires admission and intravenous therapy; AU first-line is gentamicin plus benzylpenicillin or ampicillin pending culture and sensitivity. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone monotherapy for 7 days irrespective of culture results once initial blood culture is drawn
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is reserved when first-line cover is inadequate or sepsis is severe; gentamicin and penicillin remain first-line for uncomplicated febrile UTI in young infants. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim for 7 days with renal ultrasound at 6 weeks if symptoms persist beyond 5 days of therapy
options.rationale: Trimethoprim has significant resistance and is not first-line for febrile UTI in young infants; the AU pathway requires IV therapy at this age. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral nitrofurantoin for 5 days, encouraging increased fluid intake and follow-up urine culture in 1 week
options.rationale: Nitrofurantoin does not achieve adequate tissue levels for pyelonephritis; it is reserved for older children with uncomplicated cystitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Infants under 6 months with febrile urinary tract infection require admission for intravenous antibiotics in Australia due to the risk of bacteraemia and renal scarring. First-line cover is gentamicin (single daily dose) plus benzylpenicillin or ampicillin, transitioning to a narrow-spectrum oral agent once afebrile and sensitivities known, for a total course of 7 to 10 days. A renal tract ultrasound is recommended after the first febrile UTI under 6 months; further imaging (MCUG, DMSA) depends on the ultrasound findings and clinical course.
key_points: Febrile UTI under 6 months: admit for IV gentamicin + benzylpenicillin/ampicillin
Ultrasound urinary tract after first febrile UTI in this age group
Switch to oral once afebrile and sensitivities known
Beware enteric gram-negative resistance to trimethoprim
explanation.pearls: AU first-line for febrile UTI under 6 months: IV gentamicin + benzylpenicillin/ampicillin then oral switch on sensitivities for a 7 to 10 day total course.

stem: An 8-month-old previously well boy was admitted 4 days ago with high fever and a clean-catch urine growing more than 100,000 colony-forming units per millilitre of Escherichia coli sensitive to gentamicin and cephalexin. He responded promptly to intravenous gentamicin and is afebrile on oral cephalexin to complete a 10-day course. His renal tract ultrasound performed yesterday is normal with no hydronephrosis. He is discharged tomorrow and feeding normally.

data_table.Examination: Afebrile, well, normal external genitalia, retractile foreskin, no abdominal mass, growth on the 50th centile
data_table.Investigations: Urine culture E. coli more than 100,000 CFU/mL, CRP peak 110 mg/L, creatinine 28 micromol/L, renal ultrasound normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: No further imaging; routine review by the general practitioner at 6 weeks
options.rationale: A first febrile UTI in an infant under 24 months still warrants further evaluation when the ultrasound is normal; Australian and NICE pathways recommend additional functional or anatomical imaging based on age and atypia. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: Micturating cystourethrogram before discharge while still inpatient
options.rationale: Routine MCUG is not first-line after a typical first febrile UTI that responded within 48 hours; it is reserved for atypical or recurrent UTI, or abnormal ultrasound findings. (Source: NICE NG224)

options.text: Magnetic resonance urography under sedation in the next month
options.rationale: MR urography is a problem-solving tool for complex anatomy or duplex systems; it is not part of the routine post-UTI imaging pathway in infancy. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months to assess for renal cortical scarring
options.rationale: A late DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months after a first febrile UTI in an infant under 3 years is the Australian and NICE-recommended strategy to detect renal cortical scarring after acute pyelonephritis resolves. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: Long-term low-dose trimethoprim prophylaxis without further imaging
options.rationale: Antibiotic prophylaxis after a first uncomplicated febrile UTI does not replace imaging and is itself controversial; it is considered after recurrent UTIs or in confirmed high-grade reflux. (Source: NICE NG224)

explanation.summary: After a first febrile UTI in an infant under 24 months that responds well to treatment, Australian pathways recommend renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks (or sooner if atypical) plus a DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months to detect cortical scarring. MCUG is reserved for atypical features (non-E. coli, slow response, septicaemia, raised creatinine, poor stream) or for those with abnormal ultrasound or DMSA. Atypical UTI shortens these timelines and lowers the threshold for MCUG.
key_points: First febrile UTI under 24 months: ultrasound plus delayed DMSA at 4 to 6 months
MCUG is reserved for atypical or recurrent UTI
Antibiotic prophylaxis is not routine after a single uncomplicated UTI
explanation.pearls: DMSA is deferred to 4 to 6 months after a febrile UTI because acute inflammation produces transient defects that may mimic scarring; scanning earlier risks false-positive findings.

stem: A previously well 7-week-old female infant is brought to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of fever and poor feeding. She had a normal antenatal and postnatal course. Her parents report no rash, no respiratory symptoms and no obvious source of infection. She has been breastfeeding but feeds have shortened to about 5 minutes per side.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 52, SpO2 99% RA, T 38.6°C, capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Pale but well perfused, soft fontanelle, chest clear, abdomen soft, no rash, no focal source identified
data_table.Investigations: Catheter urine sample: leukocytes 3+, nitrites positive, dipstick blood trace; urine culture pending; FBC/UEC/CRP sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge on oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg twice daily with GP review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Infants under 3 months with febrile UTI require admission and parenteral antibiotics until culture and clinical stability allow oral switch. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: Admit for IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin and complete septic workup including LP
options.rationale: Under 3 months with fever and possible UTI requires full septic workup (blood and urine cultures, lumbar puncture) and broad IV cover with benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin until cultures confirm a source. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile young infant)

options.text: Commence oral trimethoprim 4 mg/kg twice daily for 3 days and arrange renal ultrasound
options.rationale: Short oral trimethoprim courses are inappropriate for febrile UTI in infants under 3 months and miss other potential sources of sepsis. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: Repeat urine collection by clean catch and observe in short stay if afebrile
options.rationale: A catheter sample with leucocytes and nitrites is sufficient for working UTI in this age group; delaying treatment increases risk of bacteraemia or meningitis. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: Arrange outpatient micturating cystourethrogram before considering antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Imaging is part of post-treatment workup in proven first UTI under 6 months but does not delay empirical antibiotics. (Source: NICE NG224)
source_refs: NICE NG224 urinary tract infection in under 16s

explanation.summary: Febrile infants under 3 months are at substantial risk of serious bacterial infection. A positive catheter urine specimen still mandates full septic workup including lumbar puncture and broad empirical IV antibiotics (benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin per RCH and Queensland). Oral therapy is started after 48 hours of stability with confirmed urinary source and clinical improvement. Imaging (renal ultrasound) is arranged during admission; MCUG is considered for recurrent UTI, atypical organisms or abnormal ultrasound.
key_points: Under 3 months febrile UTI = admit, full septic workup including LP, IV antibiotics.
Empirical regimen: benzylpenicillin + gentamicin; cefotaxime if meningitis confirmed.
Switch to oral after 48 hours if stable and culture-sensitive.
Renal ultrasound within 6 weeks for all first UTI under 6 months.
Continue prophylactic trimethoprim only if VUR grade 3 or more confirmed.
explanation.pearls: Gentamicin once-daily dosing (7 mg/kg term, 5 mg/kg preterm) reduces nephrotoxicity risk; check trough at 48 hours before the third dose to confirm clearance.

sources.label: NICE NG224 urinary tract infection in under 16s

stem: You are the neonatal registrar called to see a 24-hour-old term boy whose mother had an antenatal anomaly scan showing bilateral hydronephrosis and oligohydramnios in the third trimester. He has so far passed only a few drops of urine; the midwife reports a palpable distended bladder. He has mild respiratory distress on room air. There is no antenatal MRI; he was delivered by spontaneous vaginal delivery in a regional centre with a paediatric service and access to telehealth subspecialty advice.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8°C, HR 160, RR 64, SpO2 95% on room air
data_table.Examination: Distended bladder palpable to umbilicus, weak urinary stream when bladder pressed, no abdominal masses, no respiratory crackles
data_table.Investigations: Bedside bladder US confirms gross bladder distension and bilateral hydroureteronephrosis; Cr 110 micromol/L, K 5.4 mmol/L, urine output last 12 h 0.5 mL/kg/h

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Pass a 6 Fr feeding tube to drain the bladder and arrange urgent paediatric urology transfer
options.rationale: Initial management of suspected posterior urethral valves is bladder drainage with a small feeding-tube catheter (Foleys may traumatise the prostatic urethra) followed by transfer for micturating cystourethrogram and cystoscopic valve ablation. (Source: APSA and ANZUNS paediatric urology guidance)

options.text: Defer any intervention and recheck serum creatinine and bladder volume in 24 hours time
options.rationale: Persistent obstruction worsens AKI, electrolyte derangement and bladder damage; drainage is required within hours. (Source: ANZUNS)

options.text: Commence IV furosemide 1 mg/kg now to maximise urine output before any catheter placement
options.rationale: Furosemide will not bypass a mechanical obstruction and can precipitate electrolyte derangement; the lesion is anatomical. (Source: ANZUNS)

options.text: Insert a 10 Fr Foley balloon catheter to maintain continuous drainage during transport in retrieval
options.rationale: A 10 Fr Foley balloon catheter can sit in the prostatic urethra and traumatise the valves; a fine feeding tube is preferred until cystoscopic assessment. (Source: ANZUNS)

options.text: Perform a single suprapubic aspiration only and discharge to outpatient urology follow up
options.rationale: A single aspiration does not provide ongoing drainage; an infant with PUV needs continuous bladder decompression and definitive surgical valve ablation. (Source: ANZUNS)

explanation.summary: Posterior urethral valves are the commonest cause of severe obstructive uropathy in newborn boys and typically present with antenatal hydronephrosis, oligohydramnios and a distended bladder with weak stream postnatally. Initial management is bladder drainage with a small feeding-tube catheter (Foley balloons can traumatise the prostatic urethra), followed by paediatric urology transfer for MCUG and cystoscopic ablation. Renal function should be monitored as nephrogenic damage may already be established.
key_points: Fine feeding-tube catheter for initial drainage, not a Foley.
Definitive treatment is cystoscopic valve ablation.
Renal failure, pulmonary hypoplasia and bladder dysfunction may persist long-term.
explanation.pearls: Antenatal bilateral hydronephrosis + oligohydramnios + male fetus = plan for postnatal PUV; have a 6 Fr feeding tube ready at birth and paediatric urology on standby.

stem: A 4-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department with 2 days of fever and reduced feeding. There are no respiratory symptoms and no rash. She is the first child of well parents, was born at term and has had no prior infections. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. A catheter specimen of urine shows leucocytes and nitrites with bacteria on microscopy.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.4 degrees Celsius, HR 170, RR 40, SpO2 98% in room air, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Alert but irritable; anterior fontanelle soft; chest clear; abdomen soft with no mass; nappy area normal
data_table.Investigations: Catheter urine: leucocytes 4+, nitrites positive, bacteria 3+; bloods: WCC 18, CRP 75, creatinine 30; blood culture pending

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 4 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days at home with GP review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with febrile UTI require initial parenteral therapy due to the risk of bacteraemia and pyelonephritis; oral therapy alone is inappropriate at this age. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg three times daily for 7 days as outpatient management today
options.rationale: Outpatient oral therapy is not appropriate for febrile UTI under 6 months; admission for IV antibiotics is required while culture and sensitivities return. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Admit for IV gentamicin 7 mg/kg daily and IV benzylpenicillin while culture results return
options.rationale: Febrile UTI in infants under 6 months requires admission for empirical IV gentamicin plus benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin per Australian paediatric guidance; transition to oral therapy follows clinical improvement and sensitivities. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Urinary tract infection in children)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: Urinary tract infection in children

options.text: Discharge with a single intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and outpatient follow-up tomorrow
options.rationale: Single-dose IM ceftriaxone with outpatient follow-up is not the standard Australian approach in young infants and risks under-treatment of bacteraemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Discharge with oral amoxicillin and arrange an outpatient renal tract ultrasound next week
options.rationale: Discharge on oral amoxicillin alone is unsafe in a young infant with febrile UTI; renal tract imaging is appropriate after acute treatment but is not the initial step. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Febrile UTI in infants under 6 months is managed with admission for empirical IV gentamicin and a beta-lactam in Australia, given the risk of bacteraemia and pyelonephritis. Oral therapy alone, single-dose IM ceftriaxone, and outpatient discharge are inappropriate at this age. Renal tract imaging is arranged after acute treatment.
key_points: Infants under 6 months with febrile UTI need admission for IV antibiotics
Empirical regimen is IV gentamicin plus benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin
Outpatient oral therapy is inappropriate due to bacteraemia risk at this age
explanation.pearls: After first febrile UTI in any child under 6 months, arrange renal tract ultrasound; consider DMSA at 4-6 months and MCUG only if there is high recurrence or imaging abnormality.

sources.label: eTG Antibiotic: Urinary tract infection in children
